blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.alcance.title: DORA and financial entities: real scope
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.alcance.p1: The DORA Regulation (EU 2022/2554) has established since 17 January 2025 a set of digital operational resilience obligations that affect virtually the entire European financial ecosystem. However, not all entities have the same obligations: DORA applies the principle of proportionality to adapt the intensity of requirements to the size, risk profile and systemic relevance of each entity.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.alcance.p2: The regulation distinguishes, broadly speaking, between entities of greater systemic significance — which must comply with the full set of requirements, including the demanding TLPT tests — and smaller entities or those with less systemic impact, which may avail themselves of a simplified ICT risk management framework defined in Article 16 of DORA. This simplified framework applies to micro-enterprises (fewer than 10 employees and less than €2 million turnover) and certain categories of smaller entities.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.alcance.p3: In practice, most financial entities with real activity in the Spanish market fall outside the simplified framework and are subject to the full set of requirements. This includes banks of any size, insurance companies, fund managers, digital payment entities and the vast majority of licensed fintech. The relevant fact is not the absolute size of the entity, but whether it is regulated and whether its ICT risks could have an impact on its customers or the stability of the financial system.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.alcance.p4: For ICT providers serving these entities, DORA also has a direct although different impact. First, providers must adapt to the contractual requirements that DORA imposes on financial entities for their contracts with ICT providers supporting critical or important functions. Second, ICT providers of greater systemic relevance may be designated as "critical" by the European Supervisory Authorities and become subject to direct supervision.

blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tipos.title: Types of financial entities affected by DORA
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tipos.intro: Article 2 of DORA lists 21 types of entities subject to the Regulation. For the Spanish market, the most relevant types are:
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tipos.bde: Entities supervised by the Bank of Spain: credit institutions (banks, savings banks, credit cooperatives, financial credit establishments), payment institutions (including those operating under PSD2), electronic money institutions and branches in Spain of financial entities from third countries.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tipos.cnmv: Entities supervised by the CNMV: investment services companies (ESI), management companies of collective investment institutions (SGIIC), pension fund managers in their investment function, trading platforms (regulated markets, multilateral trading facilities), central counterparty entities, central securities depositories, crypto-asset service providers under MiCA and regulated crowdfunding platforms.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tipos.dgsfp: Entities supervised by the DGSFP: life and non-life insurance companies, reinsurers, social welfare mutuals of significant size, and employment pension funds.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tipos.proveedores: Critical ICT providers: although not strictly financial entities, technology service providers that provide ICT services supporting critical or important functions of the above entities are subject to DORA's contractual regime. Those designated as "critical" by EBA, ESMA or EIOPA become additionally subject to the direct supervision framework of the ESAs.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.bancos.title: Specific requirements for banks and insurers
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.bancos.intro: Credit institutions and insurance companies are the entities with the greatest prior regulatory maturity in ICT matters, but DORA still represents a significant leap in the formalisation and rigour of requirements. The areas where the gap is usually greatest include:
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.bancos.gobernanza: Reinforced ICT governance: DORA requires that the governing body — the board of directors — be directly responsible for ICT risk management, not just oversight. Directors must maintain up-to-date knowledge of ICT risks and actively participate in reviewing the framework. In many medium-sized banks and insurers, ICT responsibility has historically resided at intermediate management levels without sufficient board oversight.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.bancos.marco: Integrated ICT risk management framework: banks must have a complete framework that includes: digital resilience strategy approved by the board, information security policy, ICT asset management with up-to-date inventory, risk analysis and assessment methodology, protection and prevention controls, detection and response capabilities, and recovery and communication functions. Most Spanish banks have elements of this framework under the supervision of the European Central Bank (ECB) and the Bank of Spain, but DORA requires more explicit and documented formalisation.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.bancos.continuidad: Formalised ICT continuity: ICT business continuity plans must define RTOs and RPOs for all systems supporting critical functions, and must be tested at least annually. Test results and remediation plans must be documented and communicated to the governing body.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.bancos.registro: Register of all agreements with ICT providers: DORA requires maintaining a complete and up-to-date register of all contractual agreements with ICT providers, classified by criticality. This register must be available to the supervisor at any time.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.fintech.title: Requirements for fintech and critical ICT providers
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.fintech.p1: Fintechs and neobanks have a different profile from traditional banking: they are usually more agile organisations, with a higher proportion of cloud services, less proprietary infrastructure and greater dependence on external ICT providers. This makes them vulnerable to specific risks that DORA addresses directly.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.fintech.p2: For fintechs licensed as payment institutions or electronic money institutions, DORA adds to the cybersecurity obligations of PSD2 (directly applicable) and the EBA guidelines on ICT and security risk management. The difference is that DORA is directly applicable as a Regulation, with more specific requirements and clearer penalties.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.fintech.p3: The most critical point for fintechs in DORA matters is management of ICT third-party risks. A typical fintech operates on a technology stack that includes cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP), payment platforms (Stripe, Adyen), KYC providers, cryptographic key managers and dozens of third-party APIs. DORA requires that each of these providers be identified, classified by criticality, and that contracts with critical ones include the mandatory clauses of the Regulation. For a fintech that manages these contracts informally (through the provider's standard terms and conditions), adaptation can be a significant process.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.fintech.p4: ICT providers serving financial entities — financial software companies, system integrators, technology BPO providers — must understand that DORA directly impacts them: their financial clients will require updating contracts to include DORA's mandatory clauses, and they must be prepared for audits and to provide information on their security and continuity controls. Providers that do not adapt to these requirements risk losing contracts with financial clients.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tlpt.title: TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing)
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tlpt.p1: TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) is the most demanding and novel element of DORA's testing programme. These are advanced realistic attack exercises carried out by specialised external teams (Red Team) on the entity's production systems, with authorisation from the competent supervisor and based on threat intelligence relevant to the sector.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tlpt.p2: TLPTs differ from conventional penetration tests in several fundamental aspects. First, they are executed on real production systems, not test or sandbox environments. Second, the attacking team (external Red Team) receives intelligence on the most relevant threats to the entity (from open sources, the supervisor and commercial intelligence information), so that their attacks simulate the behaviour of real, sophisticated adversaries. Third, the defending team (internal Blue Team) operates without prior knowledge that the exercise is occurring, to simulate real attack conditions.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tlpt.p3: TLPTs are based on the TIBER-EU framework (Threat Intelligence-Based Ethical Red-Teaming), developed by the European Central Bank and adopted by several European central banks, including the Bank of Spain. Entities that have previously performed TIBER-EU exercises have a significant advantage, as the methodology is compatible with DORA's TLPT requirements.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tlpt.p4: Regarding mandatory nature, TLPTs are required for the most significant financial entities — in terms of size, risk profile and systemic impact — determined by national supervisors in coordination with the ESAs. For these entities, the minimum frequency is every 3 years. Entities not subject to TLPT must nonetheless perform standard penetration tests at least annually, as part of the basic testing programme that DORA requires of all entities.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.tlpt.p5: The process of a TLPT under DORA includes three phases: preparation (scope definition, selection of Red Team provider, notification to the supervisor and obtaining authorisation), execution (the test itself, which can last several weeks) and closure (preparation of the results report, remediation plan and communication to the supervisor). Results and the remediation plan must be sent to the competent supervisor within the established deadline.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.cta2.title: Does your entity need to prepare for DORA?
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.cta2.subtitle: We design and implement the DORA compliance programme adapted to your entity.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.terceros.title: Management of third-party risks in DORA
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.terceros.p1: ICT third-party risk management is, along with TLPT, the area where financial entities find the greatest implementation difficulty. DORA introduces a very detailed regime that goes far beyond what previous EBA and ECB guidelines required.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.terceros.p2: The starting point is the register of contractual agreements with ICT providers. All entities must maintain a complete and up-to-date register of all their contracts with ICT providers, including: provider identification, service description, classification of the function supported (critical/important or not), type of data accessed and country of service location. This register must be available to the supervisor.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.terceros.p3: For contracts supporting critical or important functions, DORA establishes a set of mandatory minimum clauses that must be included in contracts (Article 30 of DORA). These clauses include: precise description of services and service levels (SLAs) with quantifiable availability objectives; audit and inspection rights for the client, its auditors and the supervisor over the provider; information security obligations aligned with the client's standards; procedures for notifying the client of ICT incidents that may affect services; service continuity obligations in case of interruption; and exit clauses with migration assistance to ensure orderly contract termination without compromising continuity.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.terceros.p4: Many existing contracts between financial entities and their ICT providers do not include all these clauses, especially contracts with large cloud and SaaS providers operating under standardised general conditions. Negotiating the update of these contracts — with providers as powerful as Microsoft Azure, AWS or Salesforce — is one of the most complex practical challenges of DORA compliance. Entities must document their negotiation efforts and, in cases where the provider does not accept the minimum clauses, escalate to the supervisor with documentation of the attempt.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.terceros.p5: DORA also regulates the concentration of risk in ICT providers. Entities must assess and manage the risk of depending on a small number of critical providers (for example, excessive concentration on a single cloud provider), and must have documented exit strategies and alternatives.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.incidentes.title: ICT incident notification under DORA
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.incidentes.p1: DORA establishes a regime of classification and notification of ICT incidents that represents the greatest operational requirement in the day-to-day life of entities. The regime is prescriptive in terms of deadlines, formats and notification channels, and its non-compliance can generate direct penalties.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.incidentes.p2: The process begins with the classification of the incident according to the harmonised criteria defined in the EBA RTS. Classification criteria include: number of customers affected, duration of interruption, geographical impact, financial impact, criticality of affected services and type of data compromised. Incidents classified as "serious" trigger the mandatory notification process to the supervisor.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.incidentes.p3: The notification schedule for serious incidents is strict: initial notification to the supervisor within 4 hours from classification of the incident as serious (and in any case within 24 hours from detection), with the information available at that time even if incomplete; intermediate report within 72 hours from the initial notification, with a more complete assessment of impact and measures taken; and final report within one month from the initial notification, with full root cause analysis, definitive impact and remediation measures implemented.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.incidentes.p4: For this process to work under pressure, entities must have prepared in advance: internal incident escalation and classification procedures, notification templates in the formats required by the supervisor, clearly defined roles and responsibilities (who decides classification, who notifies, who authorises), and communication channels with the supervisor verified and tested. Entities that have not prepared these mechanisms before suffering a real incident learn it the hard way.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.title: DORA compliance roadmap for a financial entity
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.intro: The design of a DORA compliance roadmap must start from an honest diagnosis of the entity's current state and prioritise actions based on regulatory risk and implementation effort. A typical phase structure for a medium-sized financial entity would be:
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.fase0: Phase 0 — Maturity diagnosis (4-6 weeks): assessment of the current state of DORA's five pillars: ICT risk management, incident management, resilience testing, third-party management and information sharing. Prioritisation of gaps by regulatory impact. Review of the inventory of contracts with ICT providers and preliminary criticality classification. This diagnosis is the basis for designing the compliance programme.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.fase1: Phase 1 — Governance and ICT risk framework (2-3 months): approval by the board of the digital resilience strategy and ICT risk management policy. Definition of the governance model (roles, committees, reporting). Update or creation of the ICT risk management framework with the components required by DORA. Update of the ICT asset inventory and its classification.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.fase2: Phase 2 — Incidents and testing (2-3 months): design and implementation of the ICT incident classification and notification process according to DORA RTS. Preparation of notification templates and procedures. Design of the annual basic resilience testing programme. Execution of the first tests and documentation of results.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.fase3: Phase 3 — ICT third-party management (3-4 months): complete the inventory of ICT providers and classify by criticality. Review all contracts with providers of critical functions and identify gaps against DORA's mandatory clauses. Initiate contractual update negotiations. Design the due diligence and continuous monitoring process for critical providers.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.hojaDeRuta.fase4: Phase 4 — TLPT and advanced maturity (if applicable, 3-6 additional months): for entities subject to TLPT, planning and execution of the first DORA-TLPT exercise. For all entities, implementation of DORA programme monitoring indicators, reporting to the board and preparation for supervision by the competent authorities.

blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about DORA for financial entities
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.q1: Which financial entities are obliged by DORA in Spain?
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.a1: In Spain, all entities regulated by the Bank of Spain (banks, credit institutions, payment institutions, electronic money institutions), the CNMV (investment firms, asset managers, trading platforms, MiCA crypto-asset providers) and the DGSFP (insurers, reinsurers, mutual societies, pension funds) are subject to DORA. It also applies to the ICT providers that provide services supporting critical or important functions to them, who must adapt their contracts to the Regulation's requirements.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.q2: What are TLPT tests in DORA?
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.a2: TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) are advanced resilience tests based on real threat intelligence, carried out by external Red Team specialists on the entity's production systems, with supervisor authorisation. They simulate the behaviour of real, sophisticated adversaries. They are mandatory for the most significant financial entities every 3 years, and the results and remediation plans must be communicated to the competent supervisor. They are based on the European Central Bank's TIBER-EU methodology.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.q3: Do technology providers for banks have to comply with DORA?
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.a3: Yes, but in different ways depending on their relevance. ICT providers that provide services supporting critical or important functions of financial entities must accept in their contracts the mandatory clauses that DORA imposes (audit rights, SLAs, incident notification, exit plans). Providers designated as "critical" by the ESAs become additionally subject to direct supervision. All providers serving the financial sector must review their contracts and prepare for the requirements of their regulated clients.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.q4: How does DORA affect contracts with cloud providers?
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.a4: DORA requires that contracts with ICT providers for critical functions include minimum clauses: quantifiable service levels, audit and inspection rights for the entity and its supervisors, information security requirements, incident notification procedures to the client, exit plans with migration assistance and service continuity obligations. Existing contracts that do not include these clauses must be updated. Negotiating with large cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GCP) is one of the most complex challenges of DORA compliance.
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.q5: When should financial entities perform their first DORA tests?
blog/dora-entidades-financieras.article.faq.a5: All entities subject to DORA were required to have a basic testing programme (vulnerability analyses, standard penetration tests) implemented from 17 January 2025. TLPT tests for significant entities must be performed at least every 3 years; the first rounds are ongoing during 2025-2026 for entities that did not previously perform TIBER-EU exercises. The results of all tests must be documented and communicated to the governing body.

blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.breadcrumb.current: ENS Basic: complete guide for SMEs
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.hero.label: ENS Basic · SMEs · Updated May 2026
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.hero.title: ENS Basic: the complete guide for SMEs that want to bid for the Administration.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.hero.subtitle: If your company is a technology SME that wants to access public contracts, ENS Basic is probably the entry point. This guide explains what it is, what it requires, how much it costs and how to obtain it in 3-4 months.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.hero.author: Laura Sánchez · GRC Consulting Director
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.queEs.title: What is ENS Basic and why is it the entry point for SMEs
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.queEs.p1: ENS Basic — or the Basic category of the National Security Scheme — is the ENS certification level that applies to information systems whose compromise would have a limited impact on services or citizens. It is the entry level of ENS, the least demanding of the three (Basic, Medium and High), and it applies to most small and medium-sized enterprises working with the Spanish Public Administration in support technology services.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.queEs.p2: For a technology SME considering selling services to the public sector for the first time, ENS Basic is usually the first certification you need. If your company develops software for local government, provides IT support services to public entities, offers cloud solutions for public bodies or manages Administration data in an auxiliary capacity, the Basic category is likely what applies to you.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.queEs.p3: ENS Basic is not a minor requirement: it requires implementing a significant set of organisational and technical security controls, documenting them correctly and demonstrating in an audit that they work in practice. The difference from the higher categories is in the level of requirement of each control, not the absence of controls. A company with ENS Basic has demonstrated to its public clients that it has a serious and systematic information security management.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.necesitas.title: Does your company need ENS Basic or a higher category? How to know
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.necesitas.p1: The ENS category that applies to you depends on the impact a security incident would have on the information systems that will be within the certification scope. The formal process is an asset valuation analysis in five dimensions (confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity and traceability). The result determines the category.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.necesitas.p2: As a guiding — not substitute for formal analysis — the Basic category usually applies when:
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.necesitas.list:
• Your company processes Administration data that is not particularly sensitive (administrative management data, public information, anonymised statistical data).
  • An interruption of your service would not affect the provision of essential services to citizens (they are internal support services, not citizen-facing services).
  • Your company acts as an auxiliary supplier to a public entity, not as a provider of critical infrastructure.
  • The personal data you process is not of special categories (health, ideology, biometric data).
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.necesitas.p3: If any of these conditions are not met, you may need the Medium category. The High category is reserved for critical infrastructure, national defence and essential state services.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.necesitas.p4: The safest approach is not to assume the category without analysis: there are SMEs that assume Basic when the correct category is Medium (which can cause the audit to reject the scope) and companies that assume Medium when Basic would be sufficient (wasting resources on unnecessary controls).
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.cta1.text: ENS Basic or Medium? We'll tell you for free within 24 hours. Avson GRC serves SMEs nationwide on-site and remote.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.title: ENS Basic requirements: the controls your company must implement
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.intro: Annex II of RD 311/2022 defines the catalogue of ENS security measures. For the Basic category, measures are applied at their low level (L), which is the least demanding of the three levels (L, M, H). Even so, the set of required controls is substantial:
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.marco.title: Organisational framework
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.marco.p: The organisation must have an Information Security Policy approved by management, internal security regulations (set of rules governing the use of systems by personnel), and a set of security procedures that develop the policy: incident management procedure, backup procedure, access control procedure, etc. The policy must be signed by the organisation's highest authority and reviewed at least once a year.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.riesgos.title: Risk analysis
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.riesgos.p: ENS Basic requires a formal risk analysis that identifies assets in scope, threats affecting them, the probability and impact of each threat and the treatment measures applied. The most widely used methodology in Spain is MAGERIT (developed by the Ministry of Finance), although other compatible methodologies such as ISO 27005 are accepted. The result of the risk analysis feeds the Risk Treatment Plan (PTR) and the Statement of Applicability.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.tecnicos.title: Main technical controls
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.requisitos.tecnicos.list:
• Access control: authentication of all users (passwords with minimum complexity policy), account management (onboarding, offboarding and modifications formalised), principle of least privilege (each user only has access to what they need).
  • Patch and vulnerability management: formal process of updating operating systems and applications, with maximum deadlines for critical patches.
  • Protection against malicious code: up-to-date antimalware on all equipment, control of removable devices.
  • Backups: backup policy with defined minimum frequency, storage in a separate location, procedure and records of periodic restoration tests.
  • Activity logging (logs): systems must generate access and activity logs that are kept for the minimum established period.
  • Service continuity: basic continuity plan establishing maximum recovery times (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for services in scope.
  • Communications protection: encryption of external communications (HTTPS/TLS), network protection via firewall.
  • Training and awareness: personnel with access to systems must receive information security training at least once a year, with attendance record.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.proceso.title: Step-by-step process to obtain ENS Basic
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.proceso.list:
• Initial GAP analysis (weeks 1-2): assessment of current control status against ENS Basic requirements. Identification of gaps and estimation of alignment effort.
  • Scope and category definition (week 2): identification of systems in scope, asset valuation and confirmation of Basic category through risk analysis.
  • Alignment plan (week 3): preparation of the detailed work plan with actions, owners, deadlines and resources. This is the project roadmap.
  • Control implementation (weeks 4-10): execution of the alignment plan. Preparation of documentation (policy, procedures, risk analysis, statement of applicability) and implementation of pending technical controls.
  • Staff training (week 10-11): information security training session for all personnel with access to systems in scope. Attendance record and training material.
  • Internal pre-audit (week 11-12): exhaustive review of the status of all controls, verification that documentation is complete and coherent, technical check of configurations. Remediation of any detected deviation.
  • Certification audit (week 13-14): audit by ENAC-accredited body. Documentary review and technical verification.
  • Obtaining the certificate (week 15-16): resolution of any minor non-conformities and issuance of the ENS Basic certificate with 2-year validity.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.coste.title: How much does ENS Basic cost for an SME
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.coste.p1: The total cost of the ENS Basic certification process for an SME has two components: the implementation consultancy fees and the cost of the certification body's audit.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.coste.p2: Consultancy fees depend on the starting maturity (how many controls are already implemented), the complexity of the environment (number of systems in scope, technology architecture) and the size of the organisation. As an indicative reference for an SME of between 5 and 30 employees with a standard technology environment, implementation fees usually range between €4,000 and €10,000. Companies with already mature security controls (for example, those already certified in ISO 27001 or applying good secure development practices) may be at the lower end of the range.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.coste.p3: The cost of the external audit with an ENAC-accredited body for ENS Basic in an SME is usually between €3,000 and €6,000, depending on the chosen body and the scope of the audit.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.coste.p4: The total cost of the process — consulting plus audit — for a standard SME is therefore between €7,000 and €16,000. This cost should be contextualised: the value of the first public contract that the ENS certification allows you to win or maintain is usually much higher than this investment. The cost of not having ENS when the specification requires it is losing the tender.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.cta2.text: Request a personalised quote for your SME. No obligation, within 24 hours. Avson GRC works with companies nationwide, in person and remotely.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.tiempo.title: How long does it take: realistic 3-4 month timeline
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.tiempo.p1: With the support of a specialised consultancy, the complete process from project start to receipt of the ENS Basic certificate takes between 3 and 4 months for a standard SME. This is the most common timeline in our projects at Avson GRC.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.tiempo.p2: Factors that can shorten the timeline: the company already has some existing security documentation (even if incomplete), the internal team has high availability for project tasks, and the technology environment is simple (few systems, standard cloud infrastructure).
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.tiempo.p3: Factors that can lengthen it: complex or legacy technology environment requiring significant technical updates (changing authentication systems, implementing cloud backup, deploying monitoring tools), high staff turnover during the project, or limited availability of the internal team to participate in work sessions.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.tiempo.p4: Without specialised consulting, the usual timeline extends to 9-12 months, mainly due to the learning curve in interpreting the controls of RD 311/2022 and errors in preparing documentation that must be corrected before the audit.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.declaracion.title: ENS Basic: Declaration of Conformity vs certificate
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.declaracion.p1: For ENS Basic, there is a theoretical possibility of issuing a Declaration of Conformity — a self-declared compliance document — instead of obtaining the accredited body certificate. However, in practice this option is increasingly less viable for companies that want to actively bid with the Administration.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.declaracion.p2: The reason is simple: most public procurement specifications from bodies with any level of digital sophistication already require the accredited body certificate, not the Declaration of Conformity. Even for the Basic category. Submitting the declaration when the specification requires the certificate is equivalent to direct exclusion from the tender.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.declaracion.p3: The Declaration of Conformity makes sense in very specific cases: contracts with very small administrations that explicitly accept it, or as a temporary step while the certification audit is being processed. For any company with real ambition to grow in the public market, the certificate is the correct investment.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.errores.title: The 5 most common mistakes SMEs make when seeking ENS Basic
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.errores.list:
• Assuming the category without formal analysis. Many SMEs assume the Basic category without doing an asset valuation analysis. This can lead to the audit rejecting the scope or auditors determining that the correct category is Medium, invalidating all previous work.
  • Documenting without implementing. Preparing a brilliant Security Policy and an impeccable risk analysis, but without the technical controls actually being active on the systems. Auditors check real implementation, not just documents.
  • Forgetting the backup restoration test. It is the most frequent finding in ENS audits. The backup may exist, but if it has never been tested (by restoring real data), the auditor will mark it as a non-conformity.
  • Including too many systems in the scope. Trying to cover the whole company includes more systems in the scope than necessary, increasing the work and audit complexity. A well-defined and justified scope is more efficient and easier to certify.
  • Underestimating the internal availability required. The ENS project requires internal team time: meetings, documentation review, control implementation, responding to auditor questions. Companies that do not reserve that time in the team's schedule end up delaying the project or arriving at the audit unprepared.

blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about ENS Basic for SMEs
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.q1: Can I obtain ENS Basic without external consulting?
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.a1: Technically yes, but in practice it is very difficult without previous experience in security management systems. The main obstacles are the correct interpretation of the controls of RD 311/2022, the preparation of a methodologically valid risk analysis, and the preparation of documentation with the level of detail required by auditors. Companies that try it without support usually take twice as long and many do not pass the audit on the first attempt.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.q2: Is ENS Basic valid for contracts with my city council?
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.a2: It depends on the city council and the type of contract. Large city councils already require the ENS certificate in most of their ICT tenders. Medium-sized ones are incorporating the requirement. In any case, having ENS Basic is a significant competitive advantage in any public tender, regardless of whether the specification formally requires it, as it conveys confidence and seriousness to the contracting body.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.q3: How many employees does my company need to have for ENS Basic?
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.a3: ENS does not establish any size or employee number threshold. What determines whether a company needs ENS is whether it provides ICT services to public sector bodies. We have certified ENS Basic companies from 2 employees to large corporations. Size does influence the complexity of the process: a small company with few systems in scope can complete it more quickly.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.q4: Does ENS Basic need to be renewed?
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.a4: Yes. The ENS Basic certificate is valid for 2 years. After that period, it must be renewed through a new certification audit. It is recommended to start the renewal process 3-4 months before the expiry date to avoid periods without a valid certificate that could affect active contracts or tenders.
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.q5: If I have ENS Basic, can I upgrade to Medium later?
blog/ens-basico-guia-empresas.article.faq.a5: Yes, and it is the natural path for many companies. Starting from ENS Basic and upgrading to Medium when necessary is perfectly possible. The upgrade process consists of implementing the additional controls required by the Medium category, updating documentation and undergoing a new certification audit. The documented base and controls of ENS Basic remain valid and are expanded, not replaced.

blog/ens-sector-salud.hero.label: ENS · Healthcare Sector · Updated May 2026
blog/ens-sector-salud.hero.title: ENS in the healthcare sector: which healthcare ICT providers need to get certified.
blog/ens-sector-salud.hero.subtitle: If your company develops or maintains information systems for the Spanish public healthcare system — electronic health records, hospital management, telemedicine, diagnostic imaging — ENS is not optional. This guide explains who is obliged and in which category.
blog/ens-sector-salud.hero.author: Ana Rodríguez · GRC Consultant, Healthcare Sector
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ensSanitario.title: ENS in the Spanish public healthcare system
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ensSanitario.p1: The Spanish public healthcare system — the National Health System (SNS) — is one of the most demanding environments in terms of information security: it manages health data of tens of millions of citizens, operates critical systems on which human lives depend and functions through a complex network of bodies — Ministry of Health, regional health services, hospitals, primary care centres, emergency services — that interconnect with each other and with a multitude of private technology providers.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ensSanitario.p2: RD 311/2022 regulating ENS applies to any entity, public or private, that provides technology services or solutions to the public healthcare sector. In practice, this means that virtually the entire ecosystem of ICT providers to the Spanish public healthcare sector is obliged to comply with ENS: from large companies supplying hospital management systems to SMEs developing appointment scheduling applications or electronic health record modules.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ensSanitario.p3: The autonomous communities, which manage their own health services (SERGAS, SAS, SERMAS, CatSalut, Osakidetza, etc.), have been incorporating ENS compliance as a requirement in their procurement specifications at an accelerated pace since 2023. Today it is unusual to find a significant tender in the public healthcare ICT field that does not require the ENS certificate as an admission criterion.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.proveedores.title: Which healthcare ICT providers need ENS?
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.proveedores.intro: The most frequent question we receive from healthcare sector companies is whether their specific case is really within ENS's scope. The answer depends on whether the systems they develop, maintain or operate are information systems of the public healthcare sector or are directly connected to them. The most common cases requiring ENS include:
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.proveedores.list:
• Electronic Health Record (EHR) systems: any provider of EHR software operating in public hospitals or health centres is within ENS scope, regardless of size.
  • Hospital Information Systems (HIS): comprehensive hospital management platforms covering admissions, emergencies, pharmacy, laboratory, operating theatre and billing.
  • Radiology and Medical Imaging Systems (RIS/PACS): radiology management and medical image storage systems. These are particularly critical and usually classified as Medium or High category.
  • Appointment and citizen access platforms: systems that allow citizens to book appointments, consult their health data or receive clinical reports. By providing direct access to patient data from the internet, they require especially strict security controls.
  • Telemedicine and telemonitoring systems: remote medical consultation platforms, chronic patient monitoring and home care. Their exponential growth since 2020 has made them a priority target for ENS specifications.
  • Healthcare network and telecommunications infrastructure: companies operating hospital data networks, connectivity services for health centres or corporate VPNs of regional health services.
  • Hospital cybersecurity companies: providers of SOC, vulnerability management, forensic analysis and incident response in hospital environments.
  • Cloud providers for health data: any company offering IaaS, PaaS or SaaS for the storage or processing of health data of public bodies.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.cta1.text: Does your company provide software or ICT services to the public healthcare system? Avson GRC will confirm within 24 hours whether you need ENS and in which category. We work nationwide.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.categoria.title: ENS category in the healthcare sector
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.categoria.p1: The ENS category of healthcare systems tends to be higher than in other sectors, for objective reasons related to the sensitivity of the data processed and the potential impact of a security incident.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.categoria.p2: Basic Category may apply to auxiliary systems with very limited access to health data or no patient data in scope (for example, hospital facility maintenance management systems that do not include clinical data, or internal staff training software without access to real medical records).
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.categoria.p3: Medium Category is the most common for healthcare ICT providers. It applies when a security incident — loss of confidentiality, unavailability, data manipulation — would have a serious impact on healthcare services or citizens, but not catastrophic. Includes: EHR systems in medium-sized health centres and hospitals, appointment scheduling platforms, outpatient telemedicine systems and most diagnostic support systems.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.categoria.p4: High Category applies when the impact of an incident would be very serious or could directly affect people's lives. Includes: emergency and urgent care management systems, clinical decision support systems in ICUs or operating theatres, regional or national electronic health record platforms, pharmacovigilance systems and any system whose unavailability could directly compromise urgent care provision.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.rgpd.title: ENS and GDPR in healthcare: the double obligation of healthcare providers
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.rgpd.p1: ICT providers to the public healthcare sector face a double regulatory obligation that in practice must be managed in a coordinated manner: ENS and GDPR. Health data are special category personal data under Article 9 of the GDPR, meaning that their processing is subject to stricter conditions than ordinary personal data.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.rgpd.p2: ENS and GDPR complement each other: many of the technical and organisational controls required by ENS — risk analysis, incident management, access control, encryption, backups — are also the "appropriate technical and organisational measures" that the GDPR requires of controllers and processors of health data.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.rgpd.p3: However, ENS compliance does not replace GDPR compliance. Healthcare providers must also: sign the data processing agreement with the public healthcare body (Article 28 GDPR), maintain the register of processing activities, carry out impact assessments (DPIA) for high-risk processing, and manage patient rights (access, rectification, erasure). The interaction between ENS and GDPR in the healthcare sector is complex and requires an integrated approach that avoids duplication and ensures joint compliance.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.title: Typical cases of healthcare providers that need ENS
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.hce.title: Electronic health record provider
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.hce.p: A company that develops and maintains the EHR software of a public regional hospital is processing the health data of all patients treated by that hospital. The system is critical for continuity of care: a prolonged outage would paralyse hospital activity. The resulting category is usually Medium or High. The most critical controls are: availability (backups, service continuity), access control (EHR data should only be accessible by authorised clinical staff), integrity (clinical data cannot be modified without an audit log) and encryption in transit and at rest.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.telemedicina.title: Telemedicine provider
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.telemedicina.p: A teleconsultation platform that connects patients with doctors from a regional health service processes health data over the internet, which significantly amplifies the security risk. In addition to the usual ENS controls, it must pay special attention to communications security (encryption of video consultations), robust authentication of patients and professionals, and protection of consultation records.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.ciberseguridad.title: Hospital cybersecurity company
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.casos.ciberseguridad.p: A company providing SOC (Security Operations Centre) services to public hospitals has privileged access to those hospitals' systems and data. Therefore, although its product is not a healthcare system itself, the scope of its access places it within ENS scope. Additionally, the service contract with the hospital must include ENS compliance clauses and the corresponding GDPR data processing agreement.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.cta2.text: Avson GRC has specific experience certifying ICT providers in the healthcare sector nationwide. Request a quote, response within 24 hours.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ccn.title: ENS and CCN-STIC in the healthcare sector
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ccn.p1: The National Cryptologic Centre (CCN) — the body under the CNI that technically supervises ENS — has paid special attention to the healthcare sector in its guides and publications. The CCN-STIC series includes specific guides for the security of information systems in the healthcare sector, with recommendations adapted to the particularities of the clinical environment: integration of medical devices (IoMT), identity management in high-turnover hospital environments, security in sharing medical images and protection of OT networks of life-support systems.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.ccn.p2: The CCN has also issued specific alerts on ransomware attacks on the Spanish healthcare sector — which have multiplied since 2021 — and has published incident response guides for hospitals. The critical nature of the healthcare environment and the extreme sensitivity of the data processed make the CCN consider the health sector as a top priority in its national security strategy.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.certificarse.title: How to get ENS certified as a healthcare provider
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.certificarse.p1: The ENS certification process for a healthcare ICT provider follows the same general phases as for any other provider — gap analysis, alignment plan, implementation, internal pre-audit, certification audit — but with some specific considerations for the sector.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.certificarse.p2: The first consideration is scope definition. Healthcare systems are often complex, with multiple integrations between subsystems (EHR, laboratory, pharmacy, medical imaging, billing) and connections to external systems (other hospitals, primary care, pharmacies, insurers). Defining a scope that is sufficiently representative of the real risks, but manageable in terms of implementation, requires sector experience.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.certificarse.p3: The second consideration is management of connected medical devices (IoMT). Modern hospitals have hundreds or thousands of connected medical devices on their hospital networks — patient monitors, infusion pumps, ventilators, medical imaging systems — that are part of the ENS risk environment but cannot always be updated or configured according to standard security policies. Managing these devices requires a specific approach.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.certificarse.p4: The third is service continuity. For critical healthcare systems, the service continuity plan is not a formality: it must be operational, tested and capable of maintaining patient care during an IT system outage. Continuity plans in healthcare must be integrated with the hospital's crisis management plans.

blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about ENS in the healthcare sector
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.a1: Private hospitals operating exclusively in the private sector are not obliged to have ENS. However, many private hospitals have agreements with the SNS or with autonomous communities, which may mean that their systems supporting those contracted services must comply with ENS. ICT providers that supply software to manage SNS patients will be within scope even if the hospital is private.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.q2: Which ENS category does an electronic health record provider need?
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.a2: EHR systems are usually classified as ENS Medium or High. Medium is the most common for EHRs in hospitals or health centres with significant but not catastrophic impact in case of incident. High category may apply to EHR systems supporting critical emergency services, ICUs or regional/national scale systems. The exact determination requires a formal risk analysis.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.q3: Does ENS in healthcare include patient data protection?
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.a3: ENS and GDPR are complementary but distinct frameworks. ENS regulates system security; GDPR regulates the processing of personal data. ENS compliance significantly contributes to GDPR compliance, but does not replace it: the register of processing activities, impact assessments and patient rights are GDPR elements that go beyond ENS.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.q4: How long does ENS take for a healthcare software company?
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.a4: For a healthcare software company starting from scratch, the full process usually takes between 4 and 6 months for Medium category. With Avson GRC, healthcare software companies with good secure development practices have obtained the ENS Medium certificate in 3.5 months.
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.q5: Is ENS compatible with ISO 27001 in the healthcare sector?
blog/ens-sector-salud.article.faq.a5: Yes, and the combination ENS + ISO 27001 is the most common among medium and large healthcare ICT providers. Both standards share a solid conceptual base and many controls are equivalent, allowing dual implementation projects with high efficiency. For the healthcare sector, some regional procurement managers especially value this combination as a sign of security maturity.

blog/ens-vs-iso27001.hero.label: ENS · ISO 27001 · Comparison · May 2026
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.hero.title: ENS vs ISO 27001: differences, similarities and synergy
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.hero.subtitle: The two most demanded security standards in Spain. Understanding their relationship can save you months of work and tens of thousands of euros in certification.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.complementarios.title: ENS and ISO 27001: two complementary frameworks
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.complementarios.p1: When a Spanish technology company starts exploring the security requirements demanded by its clients — especially if it has or aspires to have contracts with the Public Administration — it inevitably comes across two acronyms: ENS and ISO 27001. Often, it doesn't know the difference between them or which one it needs first.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.complementarios.p2: The good news is that they are not competitors or mutually exclusive alternatives: they are complementary frameworks with a very high degree of overlap. Understanding their relationship is the key to designing an efficient certification strategy in time and cost.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.queEsEns.title: What is ENS and who does it apply to
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.queEsEns.p1: The National Security Scheme (ENS), regulated by Royal Decree 311/2022, is the Spanish regulatory framework for information security for Public Administrations and their ICT providers. It is mandatory for any organisation providing technology services to the Spanish public sector.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.queEsEns.p2: Its main characteristics: it is mandatory (not voluntary), has national scope (only applies in Spain), is oriented to protecting the information systems of the Public Administration, and its certificates are issued by ENAC-accredited bodies under the specific CCN scheme.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.queEsIso.title: What is ISO 27001 and who does it apply to
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.queEsIso.p1: ISO/IEC 27001 (in its current 2022 version) is the international standard for information security management published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) and the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC). It is applicable to any organisation, of any size and sector.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.queEsIso.p2: Its main characteristics: it is voluntary (but widely contractually required), has international scope (recognised worldwide), is oriented to establishing, implementing, maintaining and continuously improving an Information Security Management System (ISMS), and its certificates are issued by certification bodies accredited by national accreditation bodies (ENAC in Spain, UKAS in the UK, DAkkS in Germany, etc.).
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.diferencias.title: Key differences between ENS and ISO 27001
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.diferencias.row1.label: Nature
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.diferencias.row2.label: Geographical scope

blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.diferencias.row10.iso: Positively valued in private sector and some public tenders
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.solapamiento.title: The 65% overlap: why getting certified in both is cheaper
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.solapamiento.p1: This is the most important point from a practical and economic perspective. ENS and ISO 27001 share approximately 65% of their requirements. This is not a coincidence: both frameworks draw from the same sources in terms of information security principles.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.solapamiento.list:
• Information Security Policy: both require a policy approved by management
  • Risk analysis and management: compatible methodologies and reusable documentation
  • Access control, authentication and privilege management
  • Asset management and inventory
  • Physical and environmental security
  • Communications security (encryption, network segmentation)
  • Security incident management
  • Business continuity and disaster recovery
  • Supplier and supply chain management
  • Staff training and awareness
  • Internal audit and management review
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.solapamiento.p3: The practical implication is powerful: a company that certifies in ENS Medium with Avson has, upon completion of the project, approximately 65% of the work needed for ISO 27001 already done. The documentation, technical controls and management system implemented for ENS are directly reusable for ISO 27001. Only the differential requirements need to be addressed.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.cta1.text: ENS + ISO 27001 pack: the most demanded combination by Spanish technology SMEs that want to bid and grow.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.soloEns.title: When do you only need ENS?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.soloEns.p1: There are situations where ENS is the only certification needed in the short term:
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.soloEns.list:
• Your commercial activity is exclusively or predominantly with the Spanish public sector and you have no private clients requiring ISO 27001.
  • Your company is a small SME with limited resources and you need to prioritise the certification that immediately unlocks contracts.
  • The tender you are interested in specifically requires ENS and does not mention ISO 27001.
  • You want to certify in ENS as a first step and address ISO 27001 in a second project leveraging the synergy.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.soloIso.title: When do you only need ISO 27001?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.soloIso.p1: ISO 27001 without ENS makes sense in these scenarios:
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.soloIso.list:
• Your company operates exclusively with private clients (national or international) that require ISO 27001 but you have no current or planned contracts with the Spanish Public Administration.
  • Your company has clients in multiple countries and needs a certification with international recognition that ENS cannot offer.
  • You operate in sectors such as banking, insurance, healthcare or industry where ISO 27001 is the de facto sector standard.
  • Your company is going to be acquired or raise investment and the security due diligence is done under international standards.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.losDos.title: When does it make sense to have both certificates?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.losDos.p1: The combination ENS + ISO 27001 is the most demanded by Spanish technology companies that have growth ambitions, for obvious reasons:
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.losDos.list:
• Maximum market coverage: with both certificates you can bid for any Spanish public contract (ENS) and access any private national or international client that requires ISO 27001.
  • Credibility and differentiation: having both certifications is a powerful signal of security maturity that distinguishes your company from the competition.
  • Optimised cost: thanks to the 65% overlap, the total cost of obtaining ENS + ISO 27001 jointly or sequentially is significantly lower than doing them completely independently with different consultancies.
  • Regulatory synergy: having both also facilitates compliance with NIS2, DORA and other European regulatory frameworks, as many controls are shared.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.losDos.p2: The optimal strategy for most Spanish ICT SMEs is: ENS first, ISO 27001 next. ENS immediately unlocks public contracts (there is guaranteed demand), and once obtained, the path to ISO 27001 is largely travelled.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.cta2.text: Cascade certifications: ENS in 3-4 months, ISO 27001 next in 2 additional months. All with the same guarantee.

blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.title: FAQ
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.q1: Is ENS the same as ISO 27001?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.a1: No. ENS is the Spanish regulatory framework mandatory for public bodies and their ICT providers. ISO 27001 is an international voluntary standard for any organisation. Both address information security but with different approaches and scopes. However, they share approximately 65% of their requirements.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.q2: If I have ISO 27001, do I also need ENS?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.a2: If you provide ICT services to the Spanish public sector, yes. Having ISO 27001 does not exempt you from ENS compliance. However, an organisation with ISO 27001 already has most of the ENS controls implemented, which considerably reduces the additional effort to also obtain the ENS certificate.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.q3: Which is more difficult to obtain, ENS or ISO 27001?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.a3: Depends on the starting point. ENS has more prescriptive requirements (you must implement exactly what the catalogue says according to the category), which reduces ambiguity but requires technical rigour. ISO 27001 is more flexible in how controls are implemented but requires a more mature management system. With a specialised consultancy, both are perfectly achievable in similar timelines.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.q4: In which order should I get certified?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.a4: For companies wanting to bid for the public sector, the recommended sequence is ENS first (because it immediately unlocks public contracts) and ISO 27001 afterwards (leveraging the 65% work already done). If the main objective is private international clients, it may make sense to start with ISO 27001.
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.q5: How much do you save by certifying in both at once with Avson?
blog/ens-vs-iso27001.article.faq.a5: The exact savings depend on each case, but in ENS Medium + ISO 27001 projects, the combination through our cascade certification approach typically represents a 35-45% saving compared to doing the two projects completely independently with different consultancies or at very separated times.

blog/gap-analysis-ens.hero.label: ENS · Gap Analysis · Updated May 2026
blog/gap-analysis-ens.hero.title: ENS gap analysis: the first step to know how far you are from the certificate.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.hero.subtitle: Starting an ENS project without a gap analysis is like starting a journey without knowing where you are starting from. This guide explains what the gap analysis consists of, what a good report includes and what to do with the results.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.hero.author: Laura Sánchez · GRC Consulting Director
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queEs.title: What is gap analysis in the context of ENS
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queEs.p1: ENS gap analysis — also called gap analysis or difference analysis — is the process by which the current state of an organisation's security controls is assessed and compared with the requirements established by the National Security Scheme in RD 311/2022 for the applicable category (Basic, Medium or High). The result is a detailed map of what is already complied with, what is missing and what exists but needs improvement.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queEs.p2: In practical terms, the gap analysis answers three fundamental questions. Where are we? What is the real state of security controls: what documentation exists, what technical controls are active, what security processes work in practice. Where do we need to be? What are the ENS requirements for the system category in scope. How far are we? The difference between the two previous states: the gaps, classified by security domain, severity and closure effort.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queEs.p3: It is important to distinguish gap analysis from the initial diagnosis, which is a shallower version with a high-level scope. Deep gap analysis involves reviewing real documentation, interviewing key personnel, checking system configurations and contrasting all of this against the controls of Annex II of RD 311/2022 in a systematic and exhaustive manner.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.porQue.title: Why it is the mandatory first step before any ENS project
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.porQue.p1: The most important reason is economic: without a gap analysis, it is impossible to correctly size the certification project. Companies that start ENS implementation without prior analysis systematically make one of two opposite errors: they underestimate the work (and end up with projects that take more than twice the planned timeline and with significant cost overruns) or they overestimate the gaps (and allocate resources to areas already compliant while neglecting the real gaps).
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.porQue.p2: The second reason is strategic: the gap analysis determines the real system category. Many companies assume they need one category when in fact another applies. An incorrect category valuation can lead to implementing unnecessarily demanding controls (wasting resources) or going to audit with a lower category than the system actually requires (with the risk of auditors detecting the discrepancy).
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.porQue.p3: The third reason is operational: the gap analysis produces the asset inventory and risk valuation that are the basis of the formal risk analysis required by ENS. Doing them in parallel with implementation, rather than as a starting point, generates inconsistencies that auditors detect.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.porQue.p4: Finally, the gap analysis is the basis of the Alignment Plan: the document that structures all the actions needed to close the gaps, with owners, deadlines, resources and success criteria. Without the gap analysis, the alignment plan is, at best, an estimate; at worst, a wish list disconnected from reality.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.cta1.text: Free initial diagnosis within 5 working days. Knowing where you start from costs nothing. Avson GRC works with companies nationwide, on-site and remote.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.title: What an ENS gap analysis analyses
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.intro: A complete ENS gap analysis covers three main areas of assessment:
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.sistemas.title: Systems in scope
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.sistemas.p: The first step is to precisely identify which information systems fall within the certification scope. This includes: servers (physical and virtual, on-premise and cloud), applications (in-house and third-party), databases, networks and communications, end-user devices, and external service providers that have access to those systems or the data they handle. A poorly defined scope is one of the most frequent problems: too broad generates unnecessary work; too narrow may leave out systems that auditors will require to be included.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.controles.title: Current controls
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.controles.p: For each control of Annex II of RD 311/2022 applicable to the category, the gap analysis assesses: whether the control exists and is documented, whether it exists and is documented but not consistently applied in practice, whether it exists partially (for example, the technical control exists but documentation or records are missing), or whether it does not exist at all. This distinction between "on paper" and "in practice" is critical to predicting what the audit will find.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.categoria.title: Applicable category
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.queAnaliza.categoria.p: Asset valuation and impact analysis in the five security dimensions (confidentiality, integrity, availability, authenticity, traceability) allow the system category to be determined with methodological rigour. The gap analysis validates whether the category assumed by the organisation corresponds to what would result from an objective valuation, and in case of discrepancy, recommends the correct category with the corresponding technical justification.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.informe.title: How the gap analysis report is structured
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.informe.intro: A well-structured ENS gap analysis report includes the following sections:
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.informe.list:
• Executive summary: a high-level assessment of the organisation's maturity status, the recommended category and an estimate of the total alignment effort (in months and investment level). This is the only section that management reads.
  • Inventory of systems in scope: list of assets with their valuation in each security dimension and the justification of the resulting category.
  • Control-by-control analysis: for each applicable Annex II control, current status (compliant / partially compliant / non-compliant / not applicable), description of the gap (if any) and evidence reviewed.
  • Maturity level by domain: graphical view of compliance status by control family (organisational framework, access control, exploitation, communications protection, etc.). Allows at a glance to see which domains are most mature and which require the most attention.
  • Gap classification by criticality: the identified gaps are classified into three levels — critical (sure major non-conformities in audit), important (likely minor non-conformities) and minor (observations or recommended improvements).
  • Alignment roadmap: prioritised work plan with actions to close each gap, estimated effort (hours/days), required resources (internal and external) and target deadline. This is the document that transforms the gap analysis into an actionable work plan.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.diferencia.title: Difference between basic and deep gap analysis
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.diferencia.p1: In practice, there are two modalities of gap analysis with different objectives and depth. Basic gap analysis or initial diagnosis is a high-level review carried out in 1-2 days through interviews with technical and security managers, review of existing documentation at index level (without in-depth content analysis) and an approximate assessment of maturity level by domain. Its objective is to determine whether the organisation is in a position to start the certification project immediately or whether it needs a deeper analysis. At Avson GRC, this modality is free.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.diferencia.p2: Deep gap analysis includes detailed review of all existing security documentation (not just its index), structured interviews with personnel from each area (IT, HR, operations, management), technical verification of system configurations (firewalls, active directory, backup systems, logs), complete asset valuation in all dimensions, and preparation of the full report with prioritised roadmap. This analysis can last from 3 to 10 working days depending on the complexity of the environment and is the basis on which the certification project is built.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.cta2.text: Do you have an upcoming ENS audit or a tender to meet? Our express gap analysis gives you the real state of your controls within 72 hours. Available nationwide.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.tiempo.title: How long does an ENS gap analysis take
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.tiempo.p1: The duration of the gap analysis depends mainly on three factors: the size and complexity of the technology environment in scope, the availability of the internal team to provide information and attend interviews, and the depth of the contracted analysis.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.tiempo.p2: For a technology SME with a relatively homogeneous environment (few servers, one or two applications in scope, standard cloud infrastructure), a basic gap analysis can be completed in 1-2 working days and a deep gap analysis in 3-5 days. For a medium-sized company with a hybrid environment (on-premise and cloud), multiple applications in scope, several branches or work with subcontractors, the deep gap analysis can extend to 7-12 working days.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.tiempo.p3: The most common limiting factor is not the capacity of the consulting team, but the availability of internal staff for interviews and the ease of access to documentation and systems. Organisations with orderly documentation and a dedicated technical contact person significantly shorten timelines.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.resultados.title: What to do with the results of the gap analysis
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.resultados.intro: The gap analysis report is not the destination; it is the starting map of the journey. The immediate next steps after receiving the report are:
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.resultados.list:
• Validate the category: confirm with the organisation's managers that the resulting category is correct and that the defined scope is adequate for the business objectives (planned tenders, current contracts).
  • Prioritise critical gaps: gaps classified as critical are those that will generate major non-conformities in the audit. They must be tackled first, even before having the complete alignment plan.
  • Define the Alignment Plan: using the report's roadmap as a basis, the work plan is detailed with dates, owners and resources. This plan is the work contract for the certification project.
  • Communicate to management: the executive summary of the gap analysis is the document to gain management commitment to the project: they need to understand the level of investment required, the realistic timeline and the consequences of inaction.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.avson.title: Avson's gap analysis: free in the initial diagnosis
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.avson.p1: At Avson GRC we have developed our own ENS gap analysis methodology that combines documentary review, structured interviews and automated technical verification of configurations to produce a reliable report in the shortest possible time. Our experience in more than 90 ENS certification projects has allowed us to refine the list of critical controls and the most frequent gap patterns by sector and company size.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.avson.p2: The initial diagnosis is free and without obligation: in a 2-3 hour working session with your company's technical managers, we assess the status of the most relevant controls and produce a high-level report with the recommended category, main gaps identified and an estimate of the timeline and investment to obtain the certificate. We work with companies nationwide, both on-site and completely remotely.

blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about the ENS gap analysis
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.q1: Is the gap analysis mandatory to obtain ENS?
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.a1: The gap analysis does not appear as an explicit requirement in RD 311/2022, but it is considered in practice as the indispensable first step of any serious ENS implementation project. Without it, it is impossible to know how much real work lies ahead, what resources are needed and what realistic timeline the certificate can be obtained in. Organisations that attempt to start implementation without a prior gap analysis typically incur underestimations that compromise the project.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.q2: How much does an ENS gap analysis cost?
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.a2: The cost varies depending on the size of the organisation and the complexity of the technology environment. At Avson GRC, the initial diagnosis — a high-level review of the state of ENS controls — is free and determines whether a deeper analysis is needed. For complex or hybrid environments, the deep gap analysis may have a separate cost that is usually discounted from the total price of the certification project.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.q3: Can I do the gap analysis myself without consulting?
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.a3: Technically yes, with tools like PILAR from the CCN. The problem is that without prior experience in ENS audits, it is difficult to correctly assess the real state of controls: there is a tendency to be more optimistic than reality justifies. An external gap analysis provides an objective view that significantly increases the reliability of the results and subsequent planning.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.q4: Is the ENS gap analysis equivalent to the ISO 27001 gap analysis?
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.a4: They are conceptually similar but with important differences in content. The ENS gap analysis assesses compliance against Annex II of RD 311/2022. The ISO 27001 gap analysis assesses compliance against Annex A of ISO 27001:2022. Although there is significant alignment between the two frameworks, they are not identical. If you already have ISO 27001, the ENS gap analysis usually reveals a much more advanced starting point, but there are always specific ENS gaps that must be closed.
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.q5: What happens if the gap analysis reveals many gaps?
blog/gap-analysis-ens.article.faq.a5: A gap analysis with many findings is a sign that the work ahead is significant, but it is exactly the information you needed before committing to a timeline and budget. With that clear vision, the alignment plan can be designed realistically, prioritising critical gaps. At Avson GRC we have certified companies with very low initial maturity levels in 4-5 months thanks to good post-gap planning.
