blog/iso27001-pymes.meta.ogTitle: ISO 27001 for SMEs: Practical Guide 2026 | Avson GRC
blog/iso27001-pymes.meta.ogDescription: Practical guide to getting ISO 27001 certified as an SME: requirements, costs, deadlines and mistakes to avoid.
blog/iso27001-pymes.meta.twitterTitle: ISO 27001 for SMEs: Practical Guide 2026 | Avson GRC
blog/iso27001-pymes.meta.twitterDescription: Practical guide to getting ISO 27001 certified as an SME: requirements, costs, deadlines and mistakes to avoid.
blog/iso27001-pymes.announce.text: ISO 27001 for SMEs  ·  Request a quote · Does it apply to you?  ·  Consult now →
blog/iso27001-pymes.hero.label: ISO 27001 · SMEs · Practical guide 2026
blog/iso27001-pymes.hero.title: ISO 27001 for SMEs: practical guide and most common mistakes
blog/iso27001-pymes.hero.subtitle: Many SMEs believe ISO 27001 is only for large companies. That's an expensive mistake. This guide explains how to make it viable as a small business, which mistakes to watch out for and how much it really costs.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.puede.title: Can an SME get ISO 27001 certified?
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.puede.p1: The answer is yes, absolutely. In fact, the number of Spanish SMEs certified in ISO 27001 has grown steadily over the past five years. The misunderstanding that ISO 27001 is only for large corporations originates from the — erroneous — perception that implementing 93 security controls requires a huge IT department and a million-dollar budget.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.puede.p2: The reality is different. ISO 27001 is a scalable standard: the level of implementation complexity does not depend on the size of the company, but on the scope of the Information Security Management System (ISMS) that is defined. An SME of 15 employees can perfectly well certify its software development service or its SaaS platform under ISO 27001 with a well-defined scope.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.puede.p3: What is true is that an SME needs more external help than a large company to implement ISO 27001 efficiently. Specialised knowledge in information security management is not usually present internally in small companies, and building it from scratch is prohibitively expensive. The smart alternative is to rely on a specialised consultancy.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ventajas.title: Advantages of ISO 27001 for SMEs
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ventajas.intro: SMEs that obtain ISO 27001 consistently report the following advantages:
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ventajas.list:
• Access to new clients: large clients (banking, insurance, pharmaceutical, large corporations) and international clients require ISO 27001 from their technology service providers. The certificate opens doors that were previously closed.
  • Advantage in tenders: although ENS is the formal requirement for public contracts in Spain, ISO 27001 is positively valued in many specifications and can award additional points.
  • Real risk reduction: the implementation process forces the systematic identification and management of information security risks. Many SMEs discover during the process vulnerabilities they were unaware of that could have caused serious incidents.
  • Trust of current clients: having ISO 27001 reduces the pressure from current clients to conduct internal security audits or extensive security questionnaires.
  • Improvement of internal processes: implementing ISO 27001 forces formalisation and documentation of processes that in an SME are usually implicit or in the head of a single person. This reduces dependence on specific individuals and improves operational resilience.
  • Preparation for growth: an SME with ISO 27001 is better prepared to grow: it can incorporate new clients, employees and systems without security being a brake.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.cta1.title: Is ISO 27001 viable for your SME? Request a quote: we'll tell you if it's the right time and how to do it in 3-4 months.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.alcance.title: ISMS scope: key to viability for SMEs
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.alcance.p1: The ISMS scope is the most important decision of the entire ISO 27001 certification process. It defines what part of the organisation, which services, which information systems and which assets fall within the scope of the certification.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.alcance.p2: For an SME, the temptation is to include "everything" in the scope to appear more complete. It is a strategic error. An overly broad scope multiplies the number of controls to implement, systems to protect, people to train and evidence to generate for the audit. It can triple the cost and timeline of certification.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.alcance.p3: The correct strategy for an SME is to define a delimited and well-justified scope around the services or assets of greatest value and greatest risk exposure. For example:
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.alcance.list:
• A software development company can certify exclusively its development and software delivery service, excluding administrative or HR activities.
  • An IT consulting company can certify the provision of managed services to clients, excluding its own back office.
  • A SaaS provider can certify the cloud platform it offers to clients, with the systems and processes that support it.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.alcance.p4: The scope must be documented in the ISMS Scope Statement, which is one of the mandatory documents of the standard and will be examined by the auditor. It must be consistent, justified and not leave out activities that are clearly part of the certified service.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.controles.title: The 93 ISO 27001 controls in plain language
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.controles.p1: The 2022 version of ISO 27001 reorganised its control catalogue into 93 controls distributed across 4 broad themes (in the 2013 version there were 114 controls in 14 domains). This reorganisation simplified the structure and aligned it better with modern cybersecurity frameworks.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.controles.list:
• Organisational controls (37 controls): policies, roles, responsibilities, HR security, asset management, information classification, supplier management, incident management, continuity plans, legal compliance.
  • People controls (8 controls): personnel selection, employment conditions, security training, discipline, telework, confidentiality agreements.
  • Physical controls (14 controls): physical security perimeters, physical access control, equipment protection, desk security, media protection.
  • Technological controls (34 controls): authentication, access control, encryption, vulnerability management, malware protection, monitoring, network security, change management, backup, audit logs.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.controles.p3: Not all controls are applicable to all organisations. The standard requires the preparation of a Statement of Applicability that justifies for each control whether it applies or not, and if not, the justification for its exclusion. For an SME with a limited scope, it is common for between 15% and 25% of controls to be justifiably excludable.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.title: How much does ISO 27001 cost for an SME
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.intro: The total cost of getting ISO 27001 certified has three main components:
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.p1: 1. Consulting cost: the fees of the consultancy that supports the implementation process. For an SME of 10-50 employees with a limited scope, this cost is usually between €8,000 and €25,000 depending on the starting state of existing controls, the complexity of the scope and the level of involvement required from the internal team.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.p2: 2. Audit cost: the fees of the accredited certification body that carries out the certification audit. For SMEs, this is usually between €3,000 and €8,000. The cost depends on the size of the scope and the number of audit days required.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.p3: 3. Technical implementation costs: investments in tools, licences or infrastructure necessary to implement certain technical controls (EDR solutions, password management, MFA, lightweight SIEM, encryption). For an SME, these investments are usually between €2,000 and €8,000 annually, although many controls can be implemented with existing tools or open source solutions.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.p4: In total, an SME can expect a first certification cost of between €15,000 and €40,000, with a wide range of variation depending on the starting point. The cost of annual follow-up audits (year 2 and 3) is significantly lower.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.coste.p5: An important factor: if the SME also needs or already has ENS, the combined cost of ENS + ISO 27001 using the cascade certification approach is 35-45% lower than doing the two projects independently.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.tiempo.title: How long does certification take: the step-by-step process
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.tiempo.intro: The standard ISO 27001 certification process for an SME with a limited scope has the following phases:
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.tiempo.list:
• GAP analysis (1-2 weeks): assessment of the current state against standard requirements. Identifies gaps and estimates implementation effort.
  • Scope definition and security policy (1-2 weeks): documentation of ISMS scope and preparation of the Information Security Policy approved by management.
  • Risk analysis and Treatment Plan (3-4 weeks): identification of assets, assessment of threats and vulnerabilities, risk calculation and definition of treatment controls. Preparation of the PTR.
  • Statement of Applicability (1-2 weeks): justified documentation of applicability/exclusion of the 93 controls.
  • Control implementation (4-8 weeks): development of procedures, implementation of technical controls, staff training, evidence.
  • Internal audit (1 week): exhaustive internal review before the certification audit.
  • Management review (1 session): formal management review meeting required by the standard.
  • Certification audit (Phase 1 + Phase 2, 2-4 weeks): the certification body first conducts a documentary review (Phase 1) and then an on-site audit (Phase 2).
  • Certification: issuance of the ISO 27001 certificate with 3-year validity (subject to annual follow-up audits).
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.tiempo.p1: The complete process for an SME with a limited scope typically takes between 3 and 5 months with an efficient consultancy.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.cta2.title: With Avson GRC, we guarantee certification success in writing. Even for SMEs with zero prior security experience.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.title: Most common mistakes when implementing ISO 27001 in SMEs
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.intro: These are the mistakes we repeatedly see in ISO 27001 projects in SMEs that come to us after having failed with another approach:
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.error1: Error 1: Scope too broad. Trying to certify "the whole company" on the first certification. The result is an endless and expensive project that is abandoned halfway. The solution: start with a scope limited to the most critical service or product.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.error2: Error 2: Paper documentation. Generating extensive documentation that no one reads or applies. ISO 27001 requires that controls be operational, not that they exist as documents on a server. The auditor asks for evidence of real application.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.error3: Error 3: Ignoring risk management. Treating risk analysis as an administrative formality rather than the heart of the ISMS. A superficial risk analysis produces an inadequate Risk Treatment Plan and non-conformities in the audit.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.error4: Error 4: Lack of management involvement. ISO 27001 requires real commitment from top management: approval of policies, allocation of resources, periodic review of the ISMS. An ISMS without management backing will not pass the audit.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.error5: Error 5: Forgetting maintenance. Getting certified and then not keeping the ISMS active. ISO 27001 requires annual follow-up audits (years 1 and 2) and recertification after 3 years. An ISMS that is not maintained loses its effectiveness and the certificate.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.errores.error6: Error 6: Underestimating technical controls. Believing that documentation and policies are enough. The 2022 version of ISO 27001 reinforced technological controls. Some are complex to implement: MFA, SIEM, vulnerability management, encryption in transit and at rest.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ensIso.title: ISO 27001 and ENS: the most demanded pack
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ensIso.p1: For Spanish technology SMEs, the combination ISO 27001 + ENS is the smartest certification strategy. The reasons are clear:
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ensIso.list:
• ENS is mandatory for public contracts and cannot be replaced by ISO 27001.
  • ISO 27001 opens doors in the private national and international sector.
  • Both certifications share 65% of requirements, which makes certifying in both jointly or sequentially much more efficient than doing them separately.
  • Having both certifies a complete security position that differentiates the company from competitors with only one or none.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.ensIso.p2: Avson GRC has specifically designed the ENS + ISO 27001 Pack to leverage this synergy. In a single coordinated project, the company obtains both certifications in a total timeline of 5-6 months and at a total cost lower than doing them separately with different consultancies.

blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.title: FAQ
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.q1: Can an SME of 10 employees get ISO 27001 certified?
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.a1: Yes. Size is not an impediment. ISO 27001 is scalable: the ISMS scope can be defined in a limited way to make it viable for an SME. The critical thing is to define the scope well from the beginning and not try to cover the whole organisation in the first certification.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.q2: How much does it cost to get ISO 27001 certified as an SME?
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.a2: The total cost (consulting + audit + technology) for an SME of 10-50 employees with a limited scope is usually between €15,000 and €40,000 depending on the starting point, sector and scope complexity. With Avson's ENS + ISO 27001 pack, the combined cost is significantly lower.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.q3: How much time does the internal team dedicate during the project?
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.a3: With Avson GRC, the internal team typically dedicates between 2 and 4 hours per week during the project. The bulk of the work (documentation, coordination with auditors, control tracking) is handled by the consultancy. The client validates, approves and provides system access.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.q4: Is it necessary to hire expensive technology to implement ISO 27001?
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.a4: Not necessarily. Many controls can be implemented with existing or low-cost tools. However, some important technical controls (MFA, vulnerability management, encryption) require some investment. The initial GAP analysis identifies exactly what you need and what you already have covered.
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.q5: What happens if I don't pass the certification audit?
blog/iso27001-pymes.article.faq.a5: With Avson GRC, if you don't pass the certification audit having followed our plan, we repeat the process at no additional cost. This written guarantee is possible because our audit preparation process is exhaustive and our first-attempt success rate is 100%.

blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.hero.title: ISO 27001: requirements, controls and certification process in Spain.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.hero.subtitle: Complete and updated guide for 2026: what ISO 27001:2022 requires, how many controls it has, how the audit process works and what the Statement of Applicability means.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.queExige.title: What ISO 27001 requires for certification
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.queExige.p1: ISO 27001 is the international standard for information security management. The current version — ISO/IEC 27001:2022 — was published in October 2022 and replaced the 2013 edition. To obtain certification, an organisation must demonstrate that it has established, implemented, maintained and continually improved an Information Security Management System (ISMS) in accordance with all the requirements of the standard.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.queExige.p2: Unlike other technical standards, ISO 27001 does not prescribe specific controls as mandatory for everyone. Its approach is risk-based: the organisation must identify its information assets, analyse the risks to which they are exposed, select controls proportional to those risks and demonstrate that the system works and improves. What is completely mandatory are the 10 clauses of the normative body of the standard.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.queExige.p3: The certification process culminates with an external audit carried out by an accredited certification body (in Spain, accredited by ENAC under the ISO/IEC 17021 scheme), which verifies that the organisation's ISMS meets all requirements. If the audit is satisfactory, the ISO 27001 certificate is issued, valid for 3 years with annual follow-up audits.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.clausulas.title: The 10 clauses of ISO 27001:2022
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.clausulas.p1: The normative body of ISO 27001 is structured in 10 clauses. Clauses 1 to 3 are introductory (context, terms and normative references). Clauses 4 to 10 contain the mandatory requirements:
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.clausulas.list:
• Clause 4 — Context of the organisation: Determine relevant internal and external factors, interested parties and their expectations, and define the scope of the ISMS.
  • Clause 5 — Leadership: Demonstrable commitment from top management, approved and communicated information security policy, and assignment of roles and responsibilities.
  • Clause 6 — Planning: Risk analysis and assessment process, risk treatment plan and Statement of Applicability (SoA). Setting of measurable security objectives.
  • Clause 7 — Support: Resources needed for the ISMS, staff competence, awareness, communication and management of documented information (policies, procedures, records).
  • Clause 8 — Operation: Practical implementation of risk management processes and selected controls. Change management and outsourcing.
  • Clause 9 — Performance evaluation: Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation of the ISMS. Annual internal audit and management review.
  • Clause 10 — Improvement: Management of non-conformities, corrective actions and continual improvement of the system.

blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.anexoA.title: Annex A of ISO 27001: the 93 controls
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.anexoA.p1: Annex A of ISO 27001:2022 contains the reference catalogue of security controls. In the 2022 version, this catalogue has 93 controls organised into 4 thematic categories:
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.table.r1c3: Security policies, asset management, access management, incident management, business continuity
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.table.r2c3: Personnel selection, employee responsibilities, security training, confidentiality agreements
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.table.r3c3: Perimeter security, physical access control, protection against physical threats, clear desk policy
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.table.r4c3: Logical access control, encryption, vulnerability management, network security, logging and monitoring
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.anexoA.p2: The 2022 version introduced 11 new controls compared to the previous 2013 edition, reflecting the evolution of the threat landscape: threat intelligence, cloud security, ICT continuity, physical security monitoring, configuration management, information deletion, data masking, data leakage prevention, monitoring activities, web filtering and secure coding.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.anexoA.p3: It is essential to understand that Annex A is not a checklist that must be fully complied with. The controls are for reference: the organisation selects the applicable ones based on its risk analysis and may exclude those that do not apply to its context, justifying it documentarily in the Statement of Applicability.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.sgsi.title: How an ISMS is structured
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.sgsi.p1: An Information Security Management System (ISMS) is the core of ISO 27001. It is not software or a tool: it is a management system, similar in structure to quality (ISO 9001) or environment (ISO 14001), that governs how the organisation protects its information.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.sgsi.p2: The essential components of an ISMS conforming to ISO 27001 include:
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.sgsi.list:
• Information asset inventory: Identification and classification of all assets (data, systems, software, people, facilities) that have value for the organisation.
  • Risk analysis methodology: A documented and reproducible process to identify threats and vulnerabilities, estimate the probability and impact of risks, and prioritise them. There is no mandatory methodology (MAGERIT, ISO 27005, OCTAVE or others may be used).
  • Risk treatment plan: Documented decisions on what to do with each identified risk: mitigate, transfer, accept or avoid, with specific assigned controls.
  • Statement of Applicability (SoA): The central document of the ISMS that relates all Annex A controls with their implementation status and justification.
  • Policies and procedures: The set of internal normative documentation that regulates expected security behaviour.
  • Internal audit programme: Periodic internal reviews that verify the conformity and effectiveness of the ISMS.
  • Management review: Annual formal meeting where top management assesses the state of the ISMS and makes strategic decisions.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.soa.title: The Statement of Applicability (SoA)
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.soa.p1: The Statement of Applicability (SoA) is one of the most important and characteristic documents of ISO 27001. It is, in essence, an inventory of all Annex A controls where the organisation declares:
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.soa.list:
• Whether the control is applicable or not to its context
  • Whether the control is implemented or in the process of being implemented
  • The justification for including it (what risks it mitigates) or excluding it (why it does not apply)
  • The reference to the evidence or documentation demonstrating its implementation
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.soa.p2: The SoA is a living document that evolves with the ISMS. It is one of the first documents the external auditor reviews during certification, because it allows an at-a-glance understanding of the perimeter and depth of the implemented management system. A well-built SoA greatly simplifies the certification audit.

blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.proceso.title: ISO 27001 certification process: stages and audit
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.proceso.p1: The ISO 27001 certification process has a well-defined structure that normally follows these stages:
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.proceso.list:
• Scope definition and gap analysis (weeks 1-3): Before starting the project, it is determined which systems, processes and organisational units fall within the ISMS, and the current situation is analysed against the standard's requirements to identify gaps to close.
  • ISMS implementation (weeks 4-14): Development of documentation (policies, procedures, records), implementation of technical and organisational controls, formal risk analysis, development of the SoA and staff training.
  • ISMS operation period (weeks 15-20): The ISMS must have been operational for a sufficient period before the certification audit. Normally at least 3 months of operation records are required.
  • Internal audit and management review (weeks 18-21): Mandatory requirements prior to certification. The internal audit verifies the conformity of the ISMS and the management review is the formal strategic evaluation meeting.
  • Certification audit — Phase 1 (weeks 22-23): The external auditor reviews the ISMS documentation (SoA, policies, risk analysis, internal audit records). If there are documentary deficiencies, a period is given to correct them before Phase 2.
  • Certification audit — Phase 2 (weeks 24-26): On-site audit. The auditor verifies on site that controls are implemented as documented, interviews staff and reviews evidence. At the end, a report is issued with possible minor or major non-conformities.
  • Closure of non-conformities and issuance of the certificate: If there are minor non-conformities, the organisation normally has 90 days to correct them. If the audit is satisfactory, the certification body issues the ISO 27001 certificate.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.proceso.p2: At Avson, with our intensive certification approach, we complete this entire process in 3 to 4 months for SMEs, significantly below the market average (6-12 months). Our written success guarantee covers the entire process.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.cambios.title: ISO 27001:2022 vs 2013: the important changes
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.cambios.p1: The 2022 revision introduced the most relevant changes since the publication of the standard. Organisations certified under the 2013 version had to migrate to the 2022 version before 31 October 2025. The main changes were:
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.cambios.list:
• Annex A structure: From 114 controls in 14 domains (2013 version) to 93 controls in 4 thematic categories (2022 version). Redundant controls were merged and 11 new controls were added.
  • New controls: Threat intelligence, cloud security, information deletion, data masking, data leakage prevention (DLP), physical security monitoring, monitoring activities, web filtering and secure coding.
  • Control attributes: The 2022 version adds for each control in ISO 27002 five new attributes (control type, security properties, cybersecurity concepts, operational capabilities, security domains) that facilitate their classification and selection.
  • New Clause 6.3: Planning of changes to the ISMS, aligning the standard with the Annex SL framework common to ISO management systems.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.ens.title: If you already have ENS, 65% is done
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.ens.p1: For companies that already have ENS certification, the news is excellent: the overlap between ENS and ISO 27001 is approximately 65%. This means that the documentation, technical controls and management system implemented for ENS are directly reusable or easily adaptable for ISO 27001.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.ens.p2: The ENS elements that directly cover ISO 27001 requirements include: information security policy, risk analysis and management (MAGERIT is compatible with ISO 27005), access control and privilege management, physical security, incident management, business continuity, supplier management, training and awareness, and internal audit. The remaining gap (35%) is mainly concentrated in formal management system requirements that ISO 27001 demands more structurally than ENS.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.ens.p3: In practice, Avson completes the step from ENS to ISO 27001 in approximately 6 to 8 weeks of additional work for organisations already certified in ENS Medium or High.

blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about ISO 27001
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.q1: How many controls does ISO 27001 have?
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.a1: ISO 27001:2022 has 93 controls in Annex A, organised into 4 categories: organisational controls (37), people controls (8), physical controls (14) and technological controls (34). Not all controls are mandatory for all organisations: the standard requires selecting the applicable controls based on the risk analysis and documenting the reasons for excluding those that do not apply in the Statement of Applicability (SoA).
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.q2: Which auditors certify ISO 27001 in Spain?
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.a2: In Spain, ISO 27001 certification is issued by certification bodies accredited by ENAC (National Accreditation Entity) under the ISO/IEC 17021 scheme. The main certification bodies operating in Spain include AENOR, Bureau Veritas, TÜV Rheinland, SGS, Lloyd's Register, BSI and DNV. The choice of certification body does not affect the value of the certificate, but may influence international recognition and cost.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.q3: Does ISO 27001 need to be renewed?
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.a3: The ISO 27001 certificate is valid for 3 years. During that period, the certification body conducts annual follow-up audits (generally shorter than the initial certification audit) to verify that the ISMS remains conforming and effective. In the third year, a full renewal audit is performed to obtain a new certificate for another 3 years.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.q4: How long does the ISO 27001 certification audit take?
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.a4: The duration of the certification audit depends on the size of the organisation and the scope of the ISMS. For an SME of 20-50 employees with a limited scope, the Phase 2 audit (main audit) usually lasts 1-2 days. For medium-sized organisations (50-200 employees), it can last 3-4 days. For large organisations with multiple locations, it can extend for several weeks. Previously, Phase 1 (documentary review) usually lasts half a day or a full day.
blog/iso27001-requisitos-certificacion.article.faq.a5: ISO 27001 is a voluntary standard in general. However, it may be contractually mandatory in numerous contexts: some public tender specifications require it, many contracts with large companies require it as a condition of entry into the supply chain, and sectors such as banking, insurance or healthcare consider it a de facto requirement. In the context of NIS2 and DORA, ISO 27001 certification is recognised as evidence of compliance with risk management obligations.

blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.hero.title: Business continuity plan: what it is and how to implement it in your company.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.hero.subtitle: A Business Continuity Plan (BCP) defines how your company survives a major disruption. This guide explains what it is, how to do it step by step and how it connects with ISO 22301 certification.

blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.queEs.title: What is a Business Continuity Plan (BCP)
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.queEs.p1: A Business Continuity Plan (BCP) is the document that describes how an organisation will continue operating during and after a major disruption. It is not a theoretical manual: it is an operational script with concrete instructions, identified owners, available resources and clear activation criteria.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.queEs.p2: The BCP defines, for each critical process of the organisation, what operating alternatives exist when normal means are not available. If servers are down, how are orders processed? If the head office is inaccessible, where do key employees work from? If a key input supplier fails, who is the alternative supplier and how is the contract activated? The BCP answers these questions before they happen, not during the crisis.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.queEs.p3: The term BCP is sometimes used loosely to refer to an organisation's entire continuity planning set, but strictly speaking the BCP is one of several plans that form part of a Business Continuity Management System (BCMS). The other main plans are the DRP (Disaster Recovery Plan, focused on IT recovery) and the Crisis Management Plan (focused on communication and decision-making during the incident). All three must be consistent with each other and activated in a coordinated manner.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.queEs.p4: A good BCP is not a document that is prepared once and kept in a drawer. It is a living document that must be reviewed, updated and tested periodically to ensure it remains relevant and that the personnel who must activate it know it and can execute it under pressure.

blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.porQue.title: Why your company needs a BCP
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.porQue.p1: The question is not whether your company will ever suffer a major disruption. The question is when, with what intensity and whether it will be prepared to respond. Industry data is unequivocal: according to the Business Continuity Institute, 74% of organisations suffered at least one significant business disruption in the last 12 months, and 43% of companies that experience a major disruption without a recovery plan close within the following 3 years.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.porQue.p2: The causes of disruption are more diverse than we usually imagine. Beyond spectacular natural disasters — floods, earthquakes, fires — most real disruptions have more mundane but equally devastating causes: failures in critical IT suppliers (a datacenter, an essential SaaS platform), ransomware attacks that encrypt all systems, human errors that corrupt critical databases, sudden loss of key personnel or prolonged power cuts. None of these situations are extraordinary; all are scenarios that any medium-sized company can face at any time.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.porQue.p3: Beyond operational resilience, the BCP has a growing legal and contractual dimension. The DORA regulation requires financial entities to have detailed continuity plans. The Critical Infrastructure Protection Law establishes similar obligations for critical operators. Many corporate contracts and public tender specifications now include clauses requiring proof of an up-to-date and tested BCP. In all these contexts, lacking a BCP is not just an operational risk: it is a legal and commercial risk.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.diferencias.title: Difference between BCP, DRP and BCMS
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.diferencias.intro: These three terms are often confused, even among industry professionals. Understanding their differences is essential to correctly design an organisation's continuity architecture:
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.diferencias.bcp: BCP (Business Continuity Plan): is the plan that covers the continuity of business processes as a whole. Includes people, communications, alternative facilities, manual backup processes, management of suppliers and customers during the crisis, and coordination between departments. Its scope is the business as a whole, not just technology.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.diferencias.drp: DRP (Disaster Recovery Plan): is the plan that specifically covers the recovery of IT systems and technology infrastructure. Details the procedures to restore servers, databases, applications and networks within the defined recovery times (RTO) and with the minimum possible data loss (RPO). The DRP is a technical component of the BCP, not a substitute.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.diferencias.sgcn: BCMS (Business Continuity Management System): is the complete management framework that encompasses all plans, policies, processes, roles, audits and continual improvement mechanisms of continuity. The BCMS is what ISO 22301 certifies. The BCP and DRP are components of the BCMS, along with the BIA, risk analysis, testing exercises and management review.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.diferencias.analogia: A useful analogy: if the BCMS is the road safety management system of a transport company, the BCP is the action protocol in the event of an accident, the DRP is the vehicle repair and replacement plan, and the BIA is the analysis of which routes are critical and what impact their interruption would have.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.title: How to make a business continuity plan: 6 steps
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.intro: Developing an effective BCP follows a clear methodology. These are the six fundamental steps:
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.paso1: Step 1 — Obtain management mandate: The BCP cannot be a technical project led only by IT or the security area. It requires the explicit commitment of top management, who must approve the project, allocate resources, designate a continuity officer and participate in decisions on risk tolerance. Without this mandate, the project will stall as soon as it requires resources or difficult decisions.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.paso2: Step 2 — Define scope and policy: Determine which parts of the organisation, which processes and which locations will be covered by the BCP. Develop the business continuity policy, which establishes the organisation's commitment, continuity objectives and the governance framework of the BCMS. This policy must be formally approved by management.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.paso3: Step 3 — Perform the BIA (Business Impact Analysis): This is the most critical phase. Identify business processes, determine the impact their interruption would cause over time and establish RTOs and RPOs. The BIA is the basis of all continuity strategy decisions. A well-done BIA reveals which processes are truly critical (often with surprises compared to initial assumptions) and how long their interruption can be tolerated.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.paso4: Step 4 — Analyse risks and define strategies: Identify threats that could interrupt critical processes and assess their probability and impact. For each relevant process-threat combination, design continuity strategies: alternative resources, alternative work sites, backup suppliers, reciprocal agreements, manual operation capabilities. Strategies must be realistic and financially viable.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.paso5: Step 5 — Document the plans: Draft the BCP, DRP and Crisis Management Plan. The level of detail is key: a plan too generic is useless under pressure; a plan too detailed is not updated or used. The optimal balance is action scripts with named owners, ordered steps, specific resources (not generic) and clear activation and deactivation criteria.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.comoHacer.paso6: Step 6 — Test, review and maintain: Plans must be tested before they are needed. Exercises can be walk-throughs, partial functional drills or real technical tests (system failover, alternative site activation). Each exercise must generate a report with findings and improvement actions. Plans must be reviewed at least annually and updated after any significant change in the organisation.

blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.bia.title: What is BIA (Business Impact Analysis) and how to do it
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.bia.p1: Business Impact Analysis (BIA) is the analysis that determines which business processes are critical, what impact their interruption would cause over time and what the acceptable recovery parameters are. It is the most important piece of information for designing an effective BCP.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.bia.p2: The standard BIA methodology has four phases. In the inventory phase, all business processes of the organisation are identified and assigned to an owner. In the impact analysis phase, the owners of each process are interviewed to determine the impact that its interruption would cause over time, usually at intervals of 1 hour, 4 hours, 24 hours, 48 hours, 1 week and 1 month. Impact is quantified in financial, operational, legal/regulatory and reputational dimensions.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.bia.p3: In the parameter determination phase, for each critical process the RTO (Recovery Time Objective) — the maximum tolerable interruption time before impact becomes unacceptable — and the RPO (Recovery Point Objective) — the maximum amount of data that can be lost, measured in time from the last recovery point — are established. These parameters guide the design of strategies and technical solutions: an RTO of 2 hours requires high availability solutions very different from an RTO of 24 hours.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.bia.p4: In the prioritisation phase, processes are ordered by criticality, combining the impact of their interruption and the urgency of their recovery. This prioritisation determines the order in which processes must be recovered during a real incident and concentrates continuity resources where the impact is greatest.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.bia.p5: A well-documented BIA also identifies process dependencies — which systems, data, people or external suppliers are necessary for each critical process — and the Minimum Business Continuity Objective (MBCO), i.e., the minimum level of service that must be maintained in continuity mode for the organisation to remain viable.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.iso22301.title: Business continuity plan and ISO 22301
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.iso22301.p1: ISO 22301 is the international standard that certifies that an organisation's Business Continuity Management System meets international requirements for systematic management, auditability and continual improvement. The BCP is one of the documentary products that the BCMS must generate, but the standard goes far beyond the document itself.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.iso22301.p2: ISO 22301 requires that the BCP is not just a document prepared at a given moment, but the result of a continuous management process: context analysis, risk assessment, formal BIA, documented strategies, periodically tested plans, internal audits, management review and verifiable continual improvement. The difference between having a BCP and being ISO 22301 certified is the difference between having a plan and having a management system that guarantees that plan is always relevant, tested and improved.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.iso22301.p3: For organisations that need to demonstrate to clients, regulators or auditors that their continuity management is robust, ISO 22301 certification is the most efficient way to do so. The certificate issued by an accredited body has an objective weight that no internal document can match. If your organisation already has a BCP, the path to ISO 22301 is shorter than it seems: the additional work focuses on formalising the management process, documenting the BIA and risk analyses, and evidencing exercises and tests.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.errores.title: Common mistakes when implementing a BCP
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.errores.intro: Experience in business continuity projects reveals that the same mistakes are repeated frequently. Knowing them allows you to avoid them:
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.errores.list:
• Superficial or biased BIA: the most frequent mistake is building the BIA from the office, without actually interviewing process owners. The result is an analysis that does not reflect operational reality, with overly optimistic RTOs or unidentified critical processes. The BIA requires fieldwork.
  • Plans too generic: a BCP that says "contact the IT team" without naming people, without including alternative phone numbers and without detailing exactly what they should do is useless under pressure. Plans must be executable by people who are under stress and may not have participated in their development.
  • Lack of testing: many organisations develop a BCP, approve it and never test it. When a real incident occurs, they discover the plan doesn't work: backup systems are not up to date, the alternative site does not have sufficient capacity, or employees don't know what to do. Periodic testing is essential.
  • Outdated plans: organisations change: new systems, new suppliers, reorganisations, new regulations. A BCP developed three years ago without review can be worse than having none, because it generates a false sense of security. Periodic updating is mandatory.
  • Lack of crisis communication: many BCPs focus on technical recovery and forget that during a crisis you must communicate: to employees, customers, suppliers, media and regulators. The crisis communication plan must be an explicit component of the BCP.
  • Dependence on a single person: if only one person knows and can activate the plan — the CISO, CTO or continuity officer — the organisation is exposed to a single point of human failure. The BCP must be known and practiced by multiple people in each critical area.

blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about the business continuity plan
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.q1: What is the difference between a BCP and a DRP?
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.a1: The BCP (Business Continuity Plan) covers the continuity of business processes as a whole: people, communications, facilities, alternative manual processes and relationship with customers and suppliers during the crisis. The DRP (Disaster Recovery Plan) focuses specifically on the recovery of IT systems and technology infrastructure. The DRP is a technical component of the BCP, not a substitute. A company can have an excellent DRP and lack a BCP, which means it knows how to recover its systems but not how to operate in the meantime.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.q2: How long does it take to develop a BCP?
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.a2: A basic BCP for a medium-sized organisation can be developed in 4-8 weeks with the support of a specialised consultancy. Time depends mainly on the number of critical processes to analyse, the availability of the internal team to participate in the BIA and the organisation's prior maturity in risk management. A full BCP with evidence of testing and oriented to ISO 22301 certification requires between 3 and 4 months.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.q3: Is it mandatory to have a business continuity plan in Spain?
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.a3: There is no general legal obligation to have a BCP in Spain. However, specific sectors do have obligations: financial (DORA requires continuity plans from January 2025), critical infrastructure operators (PIC Law), healthcare and public administration. Additionally, many corporate contracts and public tender specifications include proof of a current BCP as a contracting requirement.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.q4: Does the BCP need to be updated?
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.a4: Yes, with a minimum annual periodicity and whenever significant changes occur in the organisation: new critical systems, change of strategic suppliers, reorganisations, new locations or relevant regulatory changes. An outdated BCP can be worse than having none, because it generates a false sense of security and can lead to wrong decisions during a real crisis.
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.q5: What are RTO and RPO in a continuity plan?
blog/plan-continuidad-negocio.article.faq.a5: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is the maximum tolerable time that a process can be interrupted before the impact becomes unacceptable for the organisation. For example, an RTO of 4 hours for the billing system means that system must be operational within 4 hours of interruption. RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is the maximum amount of data that can be lost, measured in time: an RPO of 1 hour means that data cannot be more than 1 hour old at the time of recovery. Both parameters are defined in the BIA and determine the necessary technical solutions.

blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.hero.label: DORA · Regulation EU 2022/2554 · Mandatory from January 2025
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.hero.title: What is DORA: the European regulation that transforms digital resilience in finance.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.hero.subtitle: DORA has been mandatory since 17 January 2025 for all financial entities in the EU. This guide explains what it is, who it applies to, what it requires and how to comply efficiently.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.hero.author: By the Avson GRC team
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.toc.title: Contents of this article
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.toc.item6: Key dates: DORA is already mandatory
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.toc.item8: How to comply with DORA: roadmap
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.queEs.title: What is DORA (Regulation EU 2022/2554)
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.queEs.p1: DORA Regulation (Digital Operational Resilience Act, Regulation EU 2022/2554) is the European legal framework that establishes digital operational resilience requirements for the financial sector. It was adopted by the European Parliament and the Council of the European Union in November 2022, published in the Official Journal of the EU on 27 December 2022 and became mandatory on 17 January 2025.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.queEs.p2: DORA responds to a reality that European regulators identified as a systemic risk: the financial sector increasingly depends on complex technology systems and external ICT providers, and a technology failure in a relevant entity can have cascading consequences on the financial stability of the EU. A cyberattack on a critical payment infrastructure provider, for example, can simultaneously paralyse hundreds of financial institutions sharing that provider.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.queEs.p3: Unlike many European directives, DORA is a Regulation directly applicable in all member states, without need for national transposition. This means its requirements are identical for a Spanish, French or German financial entity: there is no room for divergent national interpretations. The technical implementation standards (RTS and ITS) developed by the European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, ESMA, EIOPA) complete the regulatory framework with specific technical requirements.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.queEs.p4: In its scope, DORA covers five major areas: ICT risk management, incident management and notification, digital operational resilience testing (including TLPT), ICT third-party risk management and information sharing on cyber threats. These five pillars form an integrated framework that goes far beyond pre-existing cybersecurity obligations.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cta1.title: Does your company need to comply with DORA?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cta1.subtitle: Request a quote within 24 hours. No obligation.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.aQuienAplica.title: Who does DORA apply to? Financial entities and ICT providers
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.aQuienAplica.p1: DORA's scope is one of the broadest in European financial regulation. Article 2 of the Regulation lists 21 types of entities subject to its requirements:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.aQuienAplica.list:
• Credit institutions (banks)
  • Investment firms
  • Payment institutions and electronic money institutions
  • Managers of alternative investment funds (AIFMs) and UCITS management companies
  • Insurance and reinsurance companies
  • Employment pension funds
  • Credit rating agencies
  • Trading platforms and market infrastructures
  • Trade repositories
  • Crypto-asset service providers (under MiCA)
  • Crowdfunding platforms
  • Information service providers
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.aQuienAplica.p2: In addition to financial entities in the strict sense, DORA includes critical ICT providers that provide services to them. These providers — which can be cloud computing companies, financial SaaS platforms, datacentre providers or software companies for financial entities — can be designated as "critical" by the ESAs and become subject to a direct supervision regime by European authorities, with inspections, information requests and direct sanctions.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.aQuienAplica.p3: The regulation includes a proportionality mechanism: micro-enterprises (fewer than 10 employees and less than €2 million turnover) are subject to a simplified version of the requirements. However, most relevant financial entities fall outside this exception.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.title: The 5 pillars of DORA
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.intro: DORA structures its requirements around five fundamental pillars that must be implemented in an integrated manner:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.pilar1: Pillar 1 — ICT risk management: entities must have a robust ICT risk management framework, with a digital resilience strategy approved by the governing body, information security policies, ICT asset management, protection and prevention, anomaly detection, response and recovery, and communication. This pillar is closest to what ISO 27001 requires in terms of security risk management.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.pilar2: Pillar 2 — ICT incident management and notification: entities must classify ICT incidents according to harmonised criteria at European level (defined in the EBA RTS) and notify serious incidents to their national supervisors within strict deadlines: initial notification within 4 hours, intermediate report within 72 hours and final report within one month. Incident management must be formalised in documented and tested procedures.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.pilar3: Pillar 3 — Digital operational resilience testing: DORA requires a continuous testing programme that includes vulnerability assessments, application security tests, configuration reviews and, for the most significant entities, TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) based on the TIBER-EU framework. TLPTs are realistic attack exercises carried out by specialised external teams with adversary knowledge.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.pilar4: Pillar 4 — ICT third-party risk management: this is possibly the most operationally demanding pillar. Entities must identify and manage all their ICT providers, classify them by criticality, have contracts with mandatory clauses defined by DORA (including SLAs, audit rights, exit plans), perform due diligence before contracting and continuously monitor the risk of critical providers. Concentration of risk in a small number of ICT providers is a special focus of supervision.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cincoPilares.pilar5: Pillar 5 — Information sharing: DORA encourages the creation of voluntary agreements for sharing cyber threat information and threat intelligence between financial entities. This sharing, when carried out under the mechanisms foreseen by DORA, does not generate additional legal liabilities for participating entities.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.title: Main requirements of DORA
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.intro: Beyond the five pillars, DORA imposes concrete requirements that every entity within its scope must implement:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.gobernanza: ICT governance: the governing body (board of directors or equivalent) is directly responsible for ICT risk management. It cannot be fully delegated to the CIO or CISO. Members of the governing body must maintain up-to-date knowledge and skills in ICT risks and actively participate in overseeing the risk management framework.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.clasificacion: Classification of ICT assets: all entities must maintain an up-to-date inventory of their ICT assets (hardware, software, applications, data) and classify them according to their criticality to business processes.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.continuidad: ICT business continuity: entities must have specific ICT continuity policies and plans, with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO) for critical systems, tested at least annually.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.pruebas: Mandatory annual tests: all entities must test their ICT systems at least annually, including vulnerability analyses, basic penetration tests and configuration reviews. Significant entities are additionally required to perform TLPT every 3 years.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.requisitos.proveedores: Management of critical ICT providers: contracts with ICT providers supporting critical functions must include minimum clauses on service levels, audit, data security, incident notification and exit plans. The entity must be able to terminate the contract without compromising service continuity.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.doraVsNis2.title: DORA vs NIS2: key differences
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.doraVsNis2.intro: Confusion between DORA and NIS2 is common, especially among financial entities wondering which standard to apply. The answer is relatively clear:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.doraVsNis2.nis2: NIS2 (Directive EU 2022/2555, transposed in Spain by the Cybersecurity Law) is a horizontal cybersecurity directive covering essential and important sectors: energy, transport, healthcare, digital infrastructure, water, public administration, postal services and the financial sector. Its level of prescription is relatively general.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.doraVsNis2.dora: DORA is a Regulation specific to the financial sector with a much higher level of detail and prescription than NIS2. It regulates not only cybersecurity but digital operational resilience as a whole: ICT risk management, continuity, resilience testing, provider management and incident notification with specific deadlines and formats.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.doraVsNis2.lexSpecialis: The lex specialis principle resolves the overlap: financial entities subject to DORA are exempt from the equivalent requirements of NIS2, given that DORA establishes more specific and demanding rules. However, there are areas where both regulations may coexist, and compliance planning must take both into account to identify synergies and avoid duplication.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.doraVsNis2.link: If you want to delve deeper into NIS2 and its impact on your organisation, you can consult our article on NIS2 for Spanish companies in the certifications section (soon also available on the blog: what-is-nis2-directive.html).
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cta2.title: Does DORA, NIS2 or both apply to you?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.cta2.subtitle: We help you identify it and design the most efficient roadmap.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.fechas.title: Key dates: DORA has been mandatory since January 2025
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.fechas.intro: DORA's regulatory calendar has followed a staggered process that is worth knowing to understand the current compliance context:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.fechas.list:
• 27 December 2022: publication of DORA Regulation (EU 2022/2554) in the Official Journal of the EU.
  • 16 January 2023: entry into force of DORA Regulation (20 days after publication).
  • 2023-2024: the European Supervisory Authorities (EBA, ESMA, EIOPA) developed the regulatory technical standards (RTS) and implementing technical standards (ITS) that specify DORA's technical requirements on ICT risk management, incident classification, TLPT tests and contractual requirements for ICT providers.
  • 17 January 2025: DORA application date. From this day, all entities within its scope must fully comply with all requirements of the Regulation and its technical standards. This is the moment when non-compliance generates sanctionable liabilities.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.fechas.p2: In practice, many financial entities are still in the process of full implementation of all requirements, especially in the most complex areas: comprehensive ICT provider management and TLPT tests. National supervisors (Bank of Spain, CNMV, DGSFP in Spain) are conducting initial supervision with a focus on assessing the state of implementation and compliance plans, rather than applying immediate sanctions. However, this informal grace period is limited and entities must move forward urgently.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.sanciones.title: Penalties for DORA non-compliance
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.sanciones.p1: DORA establishes a sanctioning regime that, although leaving margin for member states to develop national rules, includes elements directly applicable by European and national supervisory authorities:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.sanciones.p2: For financial entities, penalties for DORA non-compliance are the responsibility of national supervisors (Bank of Spain for credit institutions, CNMV for investment firms, DGSFP for insurance). The Regulation requires that member states ensure effective, proportionate and dissuasive penalties, and establishes that fines must be able to reach significant amounts depending on the type of infringement and the size of the entity.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.sanciones.p3: For designated critical ICT providers under direct supervision of the ESAs, the Regulation establishes a more detailed regime: fines of up to 1% of their average daily worldwide turnover during the non-compliance period, with a maximum of €5 million for individual fines. Additionally, the ESAs may impose periodic penalty payments of up to €100,000 per day for up to 6 months to force compliance.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.sanciones.p4: Beyond fines, potential sanctions include prohibition from providing certain services, restriction of activities, public disclosure of non-compliance (naming and shaming) and the requirement of binding remediation plans with supervised deadlines. For entities competing in regulated markets, mere publicity of a sanction for DORA non-compliance has a reputational impact that can exceed the economic value of the fine.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.hojaDeRuta.title: How to comply with DORA: roadmap
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.hojaDeRuta.intro: DORA compliance is not a one-off project: it is a transformation programme of ICT risk management that requires structured planning and a phased approach. A typical roadmap includes the following stages:
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.hojaDeRuta.list:
• Maturity diagnosis: assessment of the organisation's current state against the five pillars of DORA. Identification of priority gaps and estimation of remediation effort. The diagnosis must include a review of existing contracts with ICT providers to identify clauses that need updating.
  • Governance and mandate: obtaining formal commitment from the governing body, assignment of responsibilities (CISO role, ICT risk committee, provider management function) and approval of the ICT risk management policy.
  • ICT risk management framework: implementation or update of the ICT risk management framework, including asset inventory, risk assessment methodology, protection and detection controls, and response and recovery procedures. If the entity already has ISO 27001, this pillar is the most advanced.
  • Incident management: design and implementation of the ICT incident classification and notification process according to the harmonised criteria of the RTS, with procedures, owners and notification templates ready to be activated within regulatory deadlines.
  • Testing programme: design of the annual resilience testing plan, including vulnerability analyses, penetration tests and, if applicable, TLPT planning. Documentation of results and remediation plans.
  • ICT provider management: complete inventory of ICT providers, classification by criticality, contractual review and updating of clauses, due diligence processes for new providers and continuous monitoring of critical ones.
  • Continual improvement and reporting to supervisor: implementation of DORA programme monitoring indicators, internal reporting to the governing body and preparation for supervision by the competent authorities.

blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.title: Frequently asked questions about DORA
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.q1: Does DORA apply to my company?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.a1: DORA applies to regulated financial entities in the EU: banks, investment firms, payment institutions, electronic money institutions, fund managers, insurers, reinsurers, pension funds, credit rating agencies, crowdfunding platforms, crypto-asset service providers and the critical ICT providers that serve them. If your company is any of these entities or provides technology services that support critical functions to them, DORA very likely applies to you. Diagnosis is the first step to confirm it.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.q2: Is DORA the same as NIS2?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.a2: No. DORA (Regulation EU 2022/2554) is a standard specific to the financial sector with much more detailed and prescriptive requirements on digital operational resilience. NIS2 is a horizontal cybersecurity directive covering critical sectors more broadly. Financial entities subject to DORA are exempt from NIS2 in matters covered by DORA, according to the lex specialis principle. However, there are overlapping areas that should be mapped in the design of the compliance programme.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.q3: When did DORA come into force?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.a3: DORA Regulation (EU 2022/2554) was published in the Official Journal of the EU on 27 December 2022 and formally entered into force on 16 January 2023. However, its mandatory application — the date from which entities must fully comply with all its requirements and are exposed to sanctions — was 17 January 2025, after a 24-month transition period for implementation.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.q4: What are the penalties for DORA non-compliance?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.a4: Penalties vary according to the type of entity and the competent supervisor. For financial entities regulated in Spain, national supervisors (Bank of Spain, CNMV, DGSFP) can impose significant fines and corrective measures. For critical ICT providers under direct supervision of the ESAs, fines can reach up to 1% of their average daily worldwide turnover, with periodic penalty payments of up to €100,000 per day. Additionally, non-economic sanctions (restriction of activities, public disclosure of non-compliance) can have severe reputational impact.
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.q5: How long does it take to comply with DORA?
blog/que-es-dora-reglamento.article.faq.a5: Time depends on the maturity of the entity and its size. For a medium-sized entity starting from scratch, full compliance can take between 6 and 18 months. With the support of a specialised consultancy and leveraging synergies with previous frameworks (ISO 27001, ISO 22301), the process can be accelerated to 4-6 months for the most critical requirements. The important thing is to start with a maturity diagnosis that prioritises the most urgent gaps and allows demonstrating to the supervisor a structured compliance plan.
