blog/que-es-el-ens.hero.title: What is ENS: complete guide to the National Security Scheme
blog/que-es-el-ens.hero.subtitle: If your company provides any technology service to public bodies in Spain, ENS is not optional. This guide explains what it is, how it works and what you need to get certified.
blog/que-es-el-ens.hero.author: By the Avson GRC team
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.h2.queEs: What is the National Security Scheme
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.p1: The National Security Scheme (ENS) is the regulatory framework that establishes the minimum security principles and requirements for information systems of Spanish Public Administrations and for private companies that provide ICT services to them. Its main objective is to ensure that systems handling data or providing services to citizens and public bodies have an adequate level of protection against cyber threats.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.p2: In practical terms, ENS defines a catalogue of organisational, operational and protective security measures that any organisation within its scope must implement and maintain. These measures range from the information security policy and risk management to specific technical controls such as authentication, encryption and incident management.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.p3: ENS is not a voluntary standard or a recommendation of good practices: it is mandatory according to Royal Decree 311/2022, which repealed and replaced the previous RD 3/2010. Non-compliance has direct consequences: exclusion from public tenders, impossibility of renewing contracts with the Administration and, in some cases, legal liabilities.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.cta1.text: Not sure if ENS applies to your company? A no-obligation quote will resolve it within 5 working days.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.h2.historia: History and evolution of ENS: from RD 3/2010 to RD 311/2022
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.p1: ENS was born with Royal Decree 3/2010, of 8 January, as a result of the mandate of Law 11/2007 on electronic access of citizens to Public Services. That initial version established the foundations of the framework: security principles, system categorisation and a first catalogue of measures. It was a solid starting point for its time, but the threat landscape evolved rapidly.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.p2: In 2015, RD 3/2010 was modified by RD 951/2015, which introduced relevant adjustments to the audit procedure and expanded the scope. However, the digital transformation of public administrations, the proliferation of cloud computing, remote work and the emergence of more sophisticated threats made a deep revision necessary.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.p3: Royal Decree 311/2022, of 3 May, published in the BOE with reference BOE-A-2022-7191, completely repealed RD 3/2010 and established ENS in its current version. The most significant novelties were:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.li1: Extension of scope to private suppliers: any company providing technology services to the public sector must comply with ENS, not just the administrations themselves.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.li2: Update of the control catalogue: controls aligned with the latest threats (ransomware, supply chain attacks, cloud environment vulnerabilities) were incorporated.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.li3: New measure structure: Annex II of RD 311/2022 organises measures into three families — organisational framework, operational framework and protection measures — with low, medium and high levels according to system category.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.li4: Reinforcement of supply chain security: suppliers' suppliers may also be affected if they handle public sector data or systems.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.li5: Adaptation period: RD 311/2022 established a 24-month transitional period for adaptation, which has now ended.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.historia.p4: The CCN-STIC-800 Guide, published and periodically updated by the National Cryptologic Centre (CCN), develops the application criteria of ENS and is the fundamental technical reference for any implementation process.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.obligatorio.p1: Article 2 of RD 311/2022 delimits the scope. ENS is mandatory for:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.obligatorio.li1: Public Administrations in a broad sense: General State Administration, regional administrations, local administrations, public bodies and linked or dependent entities.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.obligatorio.li2: Private companies that provide information and communication technology services or solutions to the Spanish public sector, to the extent that those services affect the security of the Administration's information systems.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.obligatorio.li3: First and second level subcontractors when the main service requires ENS compliance.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.obligatorio.p2: The practical key for a private company is simple: if your company bids or has current contracts with public bodies in which Administration data is managed, stored or transmitted or any type of ICT services are provided, ENS applies to you. This includes software providers, system integrators, cloud service providers (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS), IT consultancies, telecommunications companies and any other technology provider.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.cta2.text: 87 companies already have their ENS certificate with Avson. Average time: 3.5 months. Success rate: 100%.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.h2.categorias: ENS categories: Basic, Medium and High
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.p1: One of the most important elements of ENS is its categorisation system. Not all information systems have the same potential impact if compromised. ENS establishes three categories based on the assessment of the impact a security incident would have on the system's security dimensions:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.li1: Confidentiality: what damage would be caused by unauthorised access to data?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.li2: Integrity: what impact would unauthorised modification of data have?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.li3: Traceability: what would be the consequences of not being able to audit who accessed what?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.li4: Authenticity: what would happen if the identity of the actors could not be verified?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.li5: Availability: what would be the impact of a service interruption?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.p2: The system category determines the level of requirement of the security measures:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.basica: BASIC Category: applies when the impact of an incident would be limited. It is the most frequent category among technology SMEs providing auxiliary services. It requires the implementation of approximately 45 security measures at their most basic levels. For this category, it is possible to issue a Declaration of Conformity without the need for an external audit, although more and more specifications require the full certificate.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.media: MEDIUM Category: applies when the impact would be serious. It is the most common category for medium-sized ICT providers and companies managing sensitive citizen data or systems supporting essential public services. It requires approximately 60 measures with stricter controls: mandatory two-factor authentication, periodic vulnerability analysis, continuous security monitoring and formalised continuity plans.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.alta: HIGH Category: applies when the impact would be very serious or catastrophic. Typical of critical infrastructure, national defence systems, essential state services or systems handling classified information. It requires all 73 measures at their most demanding levels, including advanced cryptography controls, physical network segregation, formalised incident response and regular penetration audits.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.categorias.p3: Determining the category is the first critical step of any ENS implementation process. Tools such as PILAR (developed by the CCN) facilitate this assessment through a structured analysis of assets and threats.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.h2.medidas: What security measures does ENS require
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.medidas.p1: Annex II of RD 311/2022 contains the catalogue of ENS security measures, organised into three frameworks:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.medidas.org: Organisational Framework (org): establishes the foundations of security management. Includes the Information Security Policy, security regulations, incident management procedures and staff training and awareness. These measures must be documented and approved at the highest level of the organisation.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.medidas.op: Operational Framework (op): develops security management controls on a day-to-day basis. It is structured into several subfamilies: planning (risk analysis, security architecture, acquisition of new components), access control (identification, authentication, authorisation), exploitation (asset inventory, security configuration, change management, protection against malicious code, incident management, service continuity) and external services.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.medidas.mp: Protection Measures (mp): specific controls to protect specific categories of assets: facilities and physical infrastructure, personnel management, equipment protection, communications protection (TLS/VPNs, network segmentation), media protection, application protection and information management (classification, encryption, backups).
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.medidas.p2: Each measure is applied with different intensity depending on the security level required by the system category: low level (L), medium (M) or high (H). For Basic category, only low levels are applied; for Medium, medium and low levels; for High, all levels.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.h2.diferencia: Difference between Declaration of Conformity and ENS Certificate
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.diferencia.p1: This is one of the most frequent questions among companies starting to explore ENS. There are two ways to demonstrate compliance:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.diferencia.ddc: Declaration of Conformity (DDC): is a document prepared by the organisation itself in which it declares that the systems in scope comply with ENS measures for the Basic category. It does not require an external audit. It is valid as an accreditation mechanism for Basic category systems, but has an important limitation: a growing number of public bodies and tender specifications no longer accept it and require the full certificate.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.diferencia.cert: ENS Certificate: is the document issued by a certification body accredited by ENAC (National Accreditation Entity) after a second-party audit. The process includes a documentary audit and an on-site (or remote) technical audit. It is mandatory for Medium and High categories, and increasingly required for Basic as well. The certificate is valid for 2 years, after which it must be renewed through a new audit.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.diferencia.p2: The audit body must be accredited by ENAC under the specific ENS certification scheme. The CCN publishes an up-to-date list of accredited bodies on its website.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.cta3.text: 100% success guarantee in writing. If you don't pass the audit following our plan, we repeat at no cost.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.h2.pasos: How to obtain the ENS certificate step by step
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.p1: The ENS certification process has well-defined phases:
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li1: Initial GAP analysis: assesses the current state of the organisation's security controls against the ENS requirements for the applicable category. This analysis identifies gaps and quantifies the effort needed to close them.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li2: Scope and category determination: defines which systems and services fall within the certification scope and determines the system category using the asset valuation methodology (usually with the PILAR tool or compatible methodologies).
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li3: Preparation of the Alignment Plan: based on the GAP analysis, a detailed implementation plan is designed with activities, owners, deadlines and necessary resources.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li4: Control implementation: actions of the Alignment Plan are executed: preparation of the Security Policy, Risk Treatment Plan (PTR), applicability document, operational procedures, staff training and deployment of technical controls.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li5: Internal pre-audit: before the certification audit, an exhaustive internal review is performed to identify and rectify possible deviations. This is the last chance to detect problems.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li6: Certification audit: an ENAC-accredited body conducts the audit. Includes documentary review and technical checks. If non-conformities are detected, the organisation has a period to rectify them.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.li7: Certificate issuance: once the audit is passed with no open non-conformities, the body issues the ENS certificate with a validity of 2 years.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.pasos.p2: With the support of a specialised consultancy like Avson GRC, this complete process takes between 3 and 4 months. The most determining factor of the timeline is not documentation — which can be generated efficiently using specialised tools — but the implementation of technical controls and the availability of the internal team to validate and provide evidence.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.related.title: Related articles
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.related.card1.title: Is the ENS certificate mandatory? Who must comply
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.related.card2.title: ENS vs ISO 27001: key differences and which one you need
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.related.card3.title: ENS Medium and High: additional requirements and how to get certified
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.a1: ENS (National Security Scheme) is the Spanish regulatory framework, established by RD 311/2022, that regulates the security of information systems of Public Administrations and their ICT providers. It defines the principles, requirements and security measures that are mandatory to guarantee adequate protection of systems supporting public services.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.q2: Is ENS mandatory for private companies?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.a2: Yes, for private companies that provide ICT services to Spanish public sector bodies. RD 311/2022 extends the obligation to suppliers and subcontractors. If your company sells software, cloud services, IT consulting or any technology service to a Public Administration, you need to comply with ENS in the applicable category.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.q3: How long does it take to obtain the ENS certificate?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.a3: With a specialised consultancy like Avson GRC, the complete process from diagnosis to certificate takes between 3 and 4 months. Without expert advice, it can extend to 12 months or more, mainly due to errors in scope determination, incorrect documentation generation and lack of audit preparation.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.q4: What is the difference between a Declaration of Conformity and an ENS Certificate?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.a4: The Declaration of Conformity is a self-assessed document valid only for ENS Basic. The ENS Certificate is issued by an ENAC-accredited certification body after an independent audit. Most public tenders require the full certificate, even for Basic category.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.q5: How much does it cost to obtain the ENS certificate?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.a5: Cost depends on system category (Basic, Medium or High), number of systems in scope, maturity of existing controls and size of the organisation. Avson's no-obligation quote includes a personalised economic proposal. As a reference, the cost is significantly lower than the value of the first public contract that the certification allows you to win.
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.q6: Does the ENS certificate have an expiry date?
blog/que-es-el-ens.article.faq.a6: Yes. The ENS certificate is valid for 2 years from the date of issue. After that period, it must be renewed through a new certification audit. It is recommended to start the renewal process 3-4 months before expiry to avoid periods without a valid certificate.

blog/que-es-tisax.meta.title: What is TISAX: Automotive Sector Certification | Avson
blog/que-es-tisax.meta.description: TISAX is the information security standard for the automotive supply chain. Discover assessment levels, who requires it and how to get certified.
blog/que-es-tisax.meta.ogTitle: What is TISAX: Security Certification for the Automotive Industry | Avson GRC
blog/que-es-tisax.meta.ogDescription: TISAX is the information security standard for the automotive supply chain. Assessment levels, process and how to get certified.
blog/que-es-tisax.announce.text: Does your client require TISAX?  ·  Request a quote and roadmap in 24 hours  ·  Request now →
blog/que-es-tisax.hero.label: TISAX · Automotive · Information Security · May 2026
blog/que-es-tisax.hero.title: What is TISAX and why it is mandatory for automotive industry suppliers.
blog/que-es-tisax.hero.subtitle: If you supply Volkswagen, BMW, Mercedes, Stellantis or any other European OEM, you have likely already received or will receive a request for a TISAX assessment. Here is everything you need to know.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.h2.queEs: What is TISAX (Trusted Information Security Assessment Exchange)
blog/que-es-tisax.article.p1: TISAX (Trusted Information Security Assessment Exchange) is the information security standard specific to the automotive industry. It was created to meet a very specific need: vehicle manufacturers (OEMs) and their suppliers handle extremely sensitive information — prototype data, confidential technical specifications, personal data of employees and customers, access to production systems — and needed a common and recognised mechanism to verify that the entire supply chain adequately protects that information.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.p2: TISAX is not a certification in the traditional sense. It is an assessment whose result is recorded in a centralised platform — the ENX Portal — and can be shared with multiple participants (manufacturers, Tier 1, Tier 2) without having to repeat the process for each client. This "exchange" feature is what makes it particularly efficient for a supply chain as complex and multi-level as that of the automotive industry.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.p3: The catalogue of requirements on which TISAX is based is the VDA ISA (Verband der Automobilindustrie Information Security Assessment), published and maintained by the German automotive industry association VDA. It is currently in version 6.0, which introduces significant improvements in the data protection and prototype security modules.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.h2.porQueSurgio: Why TISAX emerged and who manages it (ENX Association + VDA ISA)
blog/que-es-tisax.article.porQueSurgio.p1: Before TISAX, each car manufacturer conducted its own security audits on its suppliers. The result was an inefficient and costly system: a supplier supplying three different OEMs could receive three different audits in the same year, with different methodologies, criteria and auditors. The administrative and operational cost was enormous, and comparability between results was practically nil.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.porQueSurgio.p2: In 2017, the ENX Association — a non-profit organisation that brings together the main European OEMs and automotive suppliers — launched TISAX as a solution to this problem. The ENX Association manages the accreditation of assessment providers, administers the ENX Portal where results are recorded and shared, and supervises the evolution of the standard in coordination with VDA.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.porQueSurgio.p3: Today, TISAX is the de facto standard for information security in the European automotive supply chain, with more than 7,000 organisations registered in the ENX Portal and presence in more than 30 countries. Its adoption has grown especially among Spanish suppliers following the impetus of Volkswagen plants in Navarra, Mercedes in Vitoria, Stellantis in Vigo and Zaragoza, and Ford in Valencia.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.h2.quienNecesita: Who needs TISAX? The OEMs that require it
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.p1: The most frequent question we receive from Spanish companies is: "Does TISAX affect me?" The answer depends on whether your company is or aspires to be a supplier to car manufacturers that require the standard. The main OEMs that already require it or are in the process of making it mandatory throughout their supply chain include:
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.li1: Volkswagen Group (VW, Audi, SEAT/CUPRA, Porsche, Skoda): requires TISAX from all suppliers handling confidential group data.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.li2: BMW Group (BMW, MINI, Rolls-Royce): requires TISAX to access certain data exchange systems and for joint development projects.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.li3: Mercedes-Benz: requires TISAX for suppliers with access to prototype information and customer data.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.li4: Stellantis (Peugeot, Citroën, Opel, Fiat, Alfa Romeo, Jeep): in the process of progressive implementation in its supply chain.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.li5: Renault Group and Continental, Bosch, ZF (Tier 1): also require TISAX from their own suppliers (Tier 2 and Tier 3).
blog/que-es-tisax.article.quienNecesita.p2: In Spain, this has a direct impact on the supplier ecosystem in Navarra, Basque Country, Catalonia, Galicia, Castilla-La Mancha and Valencia, where most component suppliers for these plants are concentrated.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.cta1.text:

blog/que-es-tisax.article.diferencia.tbody: NatureSectoral assessment (not formal certification)International certificationSectoral scopeExclusively automotive sectorAny sector and organisationReference frameworkVDA ISA (partially based on ISO 27001)ISO/IEC 27001:2022Evaluating entityProviders accredited by ENX AssociationCertification bodies accredited by ENACSharing of resultsENX Portal: one result, multiple clientsIndividual certificate, must be presented to each clientValidity3 years3 years (with annual follow-up audits)RecognitionEuropean automotive industryInternational, all sectorsSpecific modulesPrototypes, vehicle data, data protectionNo specific sectoral modules
blog/que-es-tisax.article.diferencia.p1: The relationship between the two standards is complementary, not substitutive. Many automotive companies have both ISO 27001 and TISAX simultaneously, as each responds to different needs: ISO 27001 for the general market and tenders, TISAX for the automotive supply chain.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.h2.niveles: The 3 levels of TISAX Assessment (AL1, AL2, AL3)
blog/que-es-tisax.article.niveles.p1: TISAX defines three assessment levels depending on the sensitivity of the information handled by the supplier and the level of protection required by the manufacturer client:
blog/que-es-tisax.article.niveles.li1: AL1 (Assessment Level 1): Basic level for information of normal confidentiality. The assessment is carried out through a self-assessment with plausibility check by the auditor. It is the least demanding level and the fastest to obtain.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.niveles.li2: AL2 (Assessment Level 2): Standard level for highly confidential information. Includes an on-site audit at the supplier's facilities. It is the level most frequently required by OEMs and covers most common cases of information exchange.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.niveles.li3: AL3 (Assessment Level 3): Maximum level for very high confidentiality information, such as pre-production vehicle prototype data or autonomous driving system data. Requires two on-site audits carried out by independent auditors. It is the most demanding and expensive.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.niveles.p2: The OEM client specifies what level of assessment they require from their supplier. In practice, most Spanish component and engineering service suppliers must reach AL2. AL3 level is generally reserved for suppliers with access to physical prototypes or highly sensitive data related to cutting-edge technologies.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.queProtege.p1: The VDA ISA catalogue on which TISAX is based is designed to protect very specific types of critical information in the automotive sector:
blog/que-es-tisax.article.queProtege.li1: Prototype data: Technical specifications, designs, photographs and information of vehicles not yet publicly presented. A leak of this information can have a huge impact on the manufacturer's competitive advantage.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.queProtege.li2: Customer and employee data: Personal data of buyers, plant employees and supplier personnel. Especially relevant since the entry into force of the GDPR.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.queProtege.li3: Intellectual property and R&D information: Patents under development, algorithms, vehicle control software, simulation and test data.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.queProtege.li4: Production information: Capacities, prices, subcontracting agreements, vulnerabilities in the production chain.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.queProtege.li5: Connected systems data: With the increasing digitalisation of vehicles (ADAS, V2X, OTA updates), the security of telemetry data and connectivity interfaces is an area of growing relevance in TISAX audits.

blog/que-es-tisax.article.h2.proceso: How to obtain TISAX: assessment process
blog/que-es-tisax.article.proceso.p1: The process of obtaining TISAX follows a sequence defined by ENX Association:
blog/que-es-tisax.article.proceso.li1: Registration in ENX Portal: The company must register on the ENX Association portal (enx.com) and create a participant account. Here the scope of the assessment is defined (which systems, which locations, which type of information).
blog/que-es-tisax.article.proceso.li2: Selection of assessment provider: The company chooses one of the assessment providers accredited by ENX. It is advisable to carry out prior preparation consulting (gap analysis) with a specialised company like Avson before contracting the assessment.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.proceso.li3: Preparation (gap analysis and remediation): The current situation of the organisation is analysed against the VDA ISA requirements for the required assessment level. Missing controls are implemented, documentation is updated and staff is trained. This phase usually lasts between 2 and 4 months.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.proceso.li4: Assessment itself: For AL1: self-assessment with remote plausibility check. For AL2 and AL3: on-site audit at the facilities. The auditor evaluates the implementation of controls, interviews staff and verifies evidence.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.proceso.li5: Publication of the result in ENX Portal: Once the assessment is completed with a satisfactory result, the assessment provider registers the result in the ENX Portal. The provider can then share its result with the OEM clients by authorising their access.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.h2.tiempoCoste: Time and cost of TISAX assessment
blog/que-es-tisax.article.tiempoCoste.p1: The total time from the start of preparation to obtaining the result depends mainly on the level of security maturity of the organisation and the required assessment level. For a company with no prior security base:
blog/que-es-tisax.article.tiempoCoste.li1: AL1: Between 1 and 2 months of preparation + weeks for assessment. Total consulting + assessment cost: between €8,000 and €15,000.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.tiempoCoste.li2: AL2: Between 2 and 4 months of preparation + on-site audit. Total consulting + assessment cost: between €15,000 and €35,000 depending on the size of the organisation and number of locations.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.tiempoCoste.li3: AL3: Between 3 and 6 months of preparation + two on-site audits. Higher cost due to the double audit and additional required controls.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.tiempoCoste.p2: If the company already has ISO 27001 certification, times and costs are significantly reduced: ISO 27001 covers between 60% and 70% of VDA ISA controls, leaving a manageable gap that can be closed in a few weeks of additional work.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.bottomLinks.label: Related articles:
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.q1: Is TISAX the same as ISO 27001?
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.a1: They are not the same, although they are closely related. TISAX is based on the VDA ISA catalogue, which in turn is largely derived from ISO 27001. However, TISAX includes additional controls specific to the automotive sector (protection of prototypes, vehicle data, security in test facilities) that do not exist in ISO 27001. Having ISO 27001 greatly facilitates the TISAX process, but does not replace it.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.a2: TISAX assessments can only be carried out by auditors accredited by ENX Association. In Spain there are several accredited assessment providers: TÜV Rheinland, Bureau Veritas, SGS, DQS and others. The consultancy that prepares the company (like Avson) is different from the auditor who performs the assessment, although it can accompany during the process.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.q3: How long is a TISAX assessment valid?
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.a3: TISAX results are valid for 3 years. During that period, the organisation can share its results with participants registered on the ENX Portal without having to repeat the full assessment. At the end of 3 years, the assessment must be renewed.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.q4: Can I share my TISAX result with other OEMs?
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.a4: Yes, and this is one of the great advantages of TISAX compared to individual audits by each manufacturer. The assessment result is registered in the ENX Portal and the supplier can authorise several participants (manufacturers) to access its results simultaneously. This avoids having to pass multiple audits for each OEM client.
blog/que-es-tisax.article.faq.a5: TISAX includes a specific data protection module aligned with the GDPR, but does not replace compliance with the Regulation itself. The TISAX data protection module assesses whether the organisation correctly processes personal data in accordance with the principles of the GDPR. It is complementary, not substitutive.

blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.meta.description: NIS2 expands mandatory cybersecurity to thousands of companies in Spain. Find out if it affects you, what you must implement and the penalties for non-compliance.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.meta.ogTitle: What is NIS2: European Cybersecurity Directive | Avson GRC
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.meta.ogDescription: NIS2 expands mandatory cybersecurity to thousands of companies in Spain. Find out if it affects you, what you must implement and the penalties.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.announce.text: NIS2 in force  ·  Do you know if your company is affected? Request a quote  ·  Request now →
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.hero.label: NIS2 · European Directive · Cybersecurity · May 2026
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.hero.title: NIS2: what is the European cybersecurity directive and what does it require of your company.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.hero.subtitle: Thousands of Spanish companies are for the first time subject to cybersecurity obligations. Find out if your organisation is affected, what measures you must implement and how much time you have.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.h2.queEs: What is the NIS2 Directive (Directive EU 2022/2555)
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.queEs.p1: The NIS2 Directive (Network and Information Security 2), published as Directive EU 2022/2555 of the European Parliament and of the Council, is the most ambitious cybersecurity regulatory framework approved by the European Union to date. It entered into force on 16 January 2023, with the obligation for Member States to transpose it before 17 October 2024.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.queEs.p2: Its objective is to raise the general level of cybersecurity across the EU, ensuring that critical sectors and digital service providers have sufficient technical, operational and organisational measures to prevent, detect and respond to cyber incidents. NIS2 is not a voluntary standard: it is binding legislation that States must incorporate into their legal systems and affected companies must comply with under penalty of sanction.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.queEs.p3: In Spain, transposition is being carried out through the updating of Law 9/2018 on Security of Networks and Information Systems (NIS Law). The legislative process is ongoing, but the obligations of the directive will apply from the conclusion of national transposition. Companies that do not start preparing now risk facing very tight deadlines once the Spanish law is published.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.h2.diferenciaNis1: Difference between NIS1 and NIS2: what has changed
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.diferenciaNis1.p1: The first NIS Directive (2016) was an important starting point but with a very limited scope: it mainly affected operators of essential services (OES) and digital service providers (DSP) in very specific sectors, and its implementation was uneven among Member States.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.diferenciaNis1.p2: NIS2 represents a qualitative leap in four key dimensions:
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.diferenciaNis1.li1: Breadth of scope: NIS2 incorporates 10 high-criticality sectors and 7 additional important sectors, multiplying the number of affected entities. It is estimated that in Spain they will go from several hundred to several thousand companies obliged.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.diferenciaNis1.li2: Elimination of discretionary criteria: Under NIS1, States decided which entities were operators of essential services. NIS2 establishes objective size criteria (medium or large company) and sector that automatically determine inclusion.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.diferenciaNis1.li3: Management responsibility: NIS2 introduces personal responsibility for the governing bodies of affected entities. Boards of directors must approve risk management measures and can be declared responsible for serious non-compliance.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.diferenciaNis1.li4: Reinforced obligations: More structured risk management, stricter incident notification requirements (initial alert within 24 hours, full notification within 72 hours), greater emphasis on supply chain security and more robust supervision mechanisms by competent authorities.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.cta1.title: Does your company need to comply with NIS2?
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.cta1.subtitle: Request a quote within 24 hours. No obligation.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.h2.afecta: Which companies does NIS2 affect in Spain? Essential vs important entities
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.afecta.p1: NIS2 establishes two categories of affected entities with differentiated obligations and sanctioning regimes:
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.afecta.p2: Essential entities are large organisations (more than 250 employees or more than €50 million turnover) operating in the high-criticality sectors defined by the directive: energy, transport, banking, financial market infrastructures, healthcare, drinking water, wastewater, digital infrastructure, ICT service management (B2B) and space sector. Central and regional public administrations are also automatically included, regardless of size.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.afecta.p3: Important entities are medium-sized companies (more than 50 employees or more than €10 million turnover) operating in the high-criticality sectors mentioned above, and also medium and large companies in the additional sectors: postal and courier services, waste management, manufacturing/production and distribution of chemical products, food production/processing and distribution, manufacturing (medical devices, electronic equipment, machinery, motor vehicles), digital providers (social media platforms, search engines, marketplaces) and research.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.afecta.p4: The distinction is relevant because essential entities are subject to ex-ante supervision (authorities can inspect them at any time without prior incident), while important entities are subject to ex-post supervision (authorities act mainly in response to indications of non-compliance or after an incident).
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sectores.alta6: Digital infrastructure (IXPs, DNS, TLD, cloud providers, CDN, datacenter)
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sectores.adicional6: Digital providers (marketplaces, search engines, social networks)
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.p1: Article 21 of the NIS2 Directive establishes the cybersecurity risk management measures that affected entities must implement. It is not a closed checklist, but a framework of risk-based requirements that the organisation must adapt to its context. The minimum mandatory measures include:
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li1: Security policies and risk analysis: Information security policy approved by management and documented risk analysis and management process, reviewed periodically.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li2: Incident management: Procedures to detect, classify, notify and manage security incidents. Notification to the competent authority must be made within very strict deadlines: early alert within 24 hours, formal notification within 72 hours and final report within one month.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li3: Business continuity: Operational continuity and disaster recovery plans including crisis management and system and data restoration capability.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li4: Supply chain security: Assessment of the security of suppliers and subcontractors, including contractual security clauses and verification procedures.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li5: Security in system development and maintenance: Secure development practices, vulnerability management and coordinated disclosure policy.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li6: Effectiveness assessment: Procedures to regularly assess the effectiveness of implemented measures (audits, penetration tests, simulation exercises).
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li7: Basic cyber hygiene practices and training: Staff cybersecurity training and policies for the use of digital tools.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li8: Cryptography and encryption: Use of encryption to protect sensitive data at rest and in transit.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.obligaciones.li9: Access control and authentication: Identity management, multi-factor authentication and least privilege access.

blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.h2.sanciones: Penalties for NIS2 non-compliance in Spain
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sanciones.p1: NIS2 establishes a significantly more severe sanctioning regime than its predecessor. The maximum penalties vary by entity category:
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sanciones.p2: For essential entities, serious infringements can result in fines of up to €10 million or 2% of the total annual worldwide turnover of the previous financial year (the higher amount applies). For important entities, the limit is €7 million or 1.4% of annual turnover.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sanciones.p3: But economic penalties are not the only relevant thing. NIS2 introduces, for the first time in the field of European cybersecurity, the possibility for competent authorities to impose non-pecuniary measures on essential entities, including: temporary suspension of certification or authorisation of the services provided, temporary prohibition of the responsible director from exercising management functions in the organisation, and publication of the sanctioning resolution (with the consequent reputational impact).
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sanciones.p4: In Spain, the supervisory authority for most sectors will be INCIBE (for private entities) and the CCN (for public administrations and their suppliers), with coordination by the DSN in matters of crisis management.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sinergiaEns.p1: For Spanish companies that already have ENS certification, alignment with NIS2 is significantly more manageable. ENS and NIS2 share approximately 50% of their requirements and controls, since both frameworks are oriented to the protection of critical information systems and draw from the same sources in terms of cybersecurity good practices.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sinergiaEns.p2: The ENS controls directly reusable for NIS2 include, among others: the security policy approved by management, risk analysis and management (MAGERIT methodology compatible with NIS2), access and authentication controls, incident management and notification process, communications security and encryption, business continuity management, and staff training and awareness.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.sinergiaEns.p3: The gap between ENS and NIS2 is mainly concentrated in: supply chain management (NIS2 is more demanding on this point), incident notification to the competent authority within specific deadlines (the ENS protocol is different), and management responsibility requirements. A company with ENS Medium or High can complete its alignment with NIS2 in a few additional weeks of work.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.h2.hojaRuta: How to comply with NIS2: roadmap
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.p1: The path to NIS2 compliance follows a logical sequence that Avson has developed and validated in dozens of projects:
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.li1: Scope verification (weeks 1-2): Confirm whether the organisation falls within NIS2 scope based on its sector, size and activity. This step is critical to avoid both under-preparation and the cost of preparing for obligations that do not apply.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.li2: Gap analysis (weeks 2-4): Compare the organisation's current security status with the requirements of Article 21 of NIS2. If ENS or ISO 27001 exist, you start from a solid base.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.li3: Gap treatment plan (weeks 4-6): Prioritise measures to be implemented based on risk, impact and available resources. Includes timeline, owners and budget.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.li4: Implementation of measures (weeks 6-16): Deployment of technical and organisational controls: policy review, MFA implementation, improvement of vulnerability management, updating of contracts with suppliers, preparation or updating of continuity plans.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.li5: Training and awareness (ongoing): Specific NIS2 training for the board of directors and for technical and business staff.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.hojaRuta.li6: Audit and continual improvement: Establishment of a periodic review cycle of the effectiveness of measures and a documented continual improvement process.

blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.related.label: Related articles:
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.q1: Is NIS2 already mandatory in Spain?
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.a1: The NIS2 Directive (EU 2022/2555) entered into force on 16 January 2023 and Member States had to transpose it by 17 October 2024. Spain is in the process of legislative transposition through the updating of the Network and Information Systems Security Law. Although the final Spanish law is still in parliamentary process, affected companies should prepare now to comply from the entry into force of the national standard.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.q2: What is the difference between NIS2 and ENS?
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.a2: ENS (National Security Scheme) is the Spanish framework mandatory for public bodies and their ICT providers. NIS2 is a European directive that expands cybersecurity obligations to operators in critical sectors (energy, transport, banking, healthcare, etc.), both public and private. They share approximately 50% of their controls, so having ENS covers half the path to NIS2.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.q3: What happens if I don't comply with NIS2?
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.a3: Penalties for NIS2 non-compliance are significant: up to €10 million or 2% of annual worldwide turnover for essential entities, and up to €7 million or 1.4% of turnover for important entities. Additionally, directors may incur personal liability and the company may be excluded from tenders and contracts with the public sector.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.a4: NIS2 mainly affects medium-sized companies (more than 50 employees or more than €10 million turnover) and large companies in regulated sectors. However, small companies operating in critical sectors or that are key suppliers of essential infrastructure may also be included. It is essential to do a case-by-case verification.
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.q5: How long does it take to comply with NIS2?
blog/que-es-nis2-directiva.article.faq.a5: With a specialised consultancy like Avson, the NIS2 alignment process takes between 3 and 5 months depending on the organisation's starting point. If the company already has ENS or ISO 27001 certification, the process is significantly reduced thanks to the overlap of controls. Without a prior base, the process includes gap analysis, control implementation, training and documentation.

blog/que-es-iso22301.meta.description: ISO 22301 is the international business continuity standard. Find out what it is, what it is for, who should get certified and how to obtain it in Spain. Complete guide 2026.
blog/que-es-iso22301.meta.ogTitle: What is ISO 22301: Complete Guide to Business Continuity 2026 | Avson GRC
blog/que-es-iso22301.meta.ogDescription: ISO 22301 is the international business continuity standard. Find out what it is, what it is for, who should get certified and how to obtain it in Spain. Complete guide 2026.
blog/que-es-iso22301.announce.text: New ENS regulations in force — Find out if your company is obliged →
blog/que-es-iso22301.hero.label: ISO 22301 · Business Continuity · Updated May 2026
blog/que-es-iso22301.hero.title: What is ISO 22301: the business continuity standard explained step by step.
blog/que-es-iso22301.hero.subtitle: ISO 22301 is the international standard that defines how to implement and certify a Business Continuity Management System. This guide explains what it requires, who should get certified and how the process works in Spain.
blog/que-es-iso22301.hero.author: By the Avson GRC team
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.toc.title: Contents of this article
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.toc.items:
• What is ISO 22301
  • What it is for: objectives of the BCMS
  • Who should get certified?
  • Main requirements: the 10 clauses
  • Difference between ISO 22301 and ISO 27001
  • ISO 22301 and ENS: 55% synergy
  • How to obtain certification in Spain
  • Time and cost of certification
  • FAQ
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.queEs.p1: ISO 22301 is the international standard published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) that specifies the requirements to plan, establish, implement, operate, monitor, review, maintain and continually improve a Business Continuity Management System (BCMS). Its most recent version is ISO 22301:2019, which replaced the 2012 edition with a high-level structure aligned with other ISO management standards.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.queEs.p2: In practical terms, the standard answers a fundamental question: what would your organisation do if it stopped working for hours, days or weeks tomorrow? It is not a theoretical exercise. Incidents that disrupt operations are inevitable: ransomware attacks, failures in critical suppliers, fires, floods, power cuts, pandemics. ISO 22301 forces organisations to have concrete, tested and documented responses before those events occur.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.queEs.p3: The standard belongs to the family of ISO management system standards and shares the high-level structure (HLS) with ISO 27001, ISO 9001 and ISO 14001. This means its clauses follow the same Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) scheme, which facilitates integration with other management systems already implemented in the organisation. If a company already has ISO 27001, the learning curve for ISO 22301 is significantly lower.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.queEs.p4: The official term in Spanish is Gestión de la Continuidad del Negocio (GCN), although in practice Business Continuity Management (BCM), business continuity, operational continuity and crisis management are used interchangeably. ISO 22301 covers all these concepts within an integrated and auditable framework.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.cta1.title: Do you need ISO 22301 certification?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.cta1.subtitle: Request a quote within 24 hours. No obligation.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.h2.paraQueSirve: What ISO 22301 is for: objectives of the BCMS
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.paraQueSirve.p1: The central objective of ISO 22301 is to ensure that an organisation can continue operating during and after a major disruption, protecting its employees, customers, assets and reputation. But beyond this general objective, the standard pursues concrete and measurable results that certification accredits to third parties.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.paraQueSirve.p2: First, the BCMS helps the organisation identify its critical processes and understand exactly what would stop working and with what impact if each process were interrupted. This analysis, known as BIA (Business Impact Analysis), often reveals hidden dependencies and single points of failure that no other management process had detected before.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.paraQueSirve.p3: Second, ISO 22301 forces the establishment of continuity and recovery strategies for each critical process. This includes manual or remote operation alternatives, agreements with alternative suppliers, backup sites, data replication systems and crisis communication plans. The standard does not prescribe specific solutions; it requires that solutions exist appropriate to the context and risk level of each organisation.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.paraQueSirve.p4: Third, the standard requires that these strategies be translated into documented and periodically tested plans through exercises and drills. A plan that has never been tested is a plan that will likely fail when most needed. The cadence of tests and the records of their results are one of the most reviewed points in certification audits.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.paraQueSirve.p5: Finally, ISO 22301 certification serves as a signal of trust to customers, partners, regulators and investors. It accredits that the organisation has subjected its continuity system to an independent audit and that it meets the most demanding international standards. In sectors such as finance, healthcare, technology and public administration, this accreditation carries increasing weight in contracting and contract renewal decisions.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.quienDebe.p1: ISO 22301 is applicable to any organisation, regardless of its size, sector or legal nature. However, in practice, pressure to get certified comes from four main sources that are worth knowing:
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.quienDebe.p2: Sectoral regulation: the financial sector is the most active in this regard. The DORA regulation (Digital Operational Resilience Act), in force since January 2025, requires banks, insurers, asset managers and fintech to have continuity and operational resilience management systems that are, in practice, equivalent to what ISO 22301 requires. The PIC Law (Critical Infrastructure Protection) establishes similar obligations for operators of critical infrastructure in sectors such as energy, transport, water and healthcare.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.quienDebe.p3: Contractual requirements: many large companies and corporate groups impose on their strategic suppliers the obligation to be certified in ISO 22301. If one of your most important clients suffers a disruption caused by a failure in your supply chain, the legal and reputational consequences can be devastating. Certification is the most efficient way to demonstrate that your continuity is rigorously managed.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.quienDebe.p4: Public tenders: specifications of public contracts in critical sectors (ICT, healthcare, infrastructure) frequently incorporate ISO 22301 as an evaluation criterion or minimum requirement. As with ENS, certification becomes a commercial asset necessary to compete in certain markets.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.quienDebe.p5: Strategic decision: organisations that have experienced a major disruption or that operate in high-risk exposure environments (dependence on a single datacenter, concentrated supplier, geographical area prone to natural disasters) decide to certify as part of their organisational maturity, without waiting for a regulator or client to require it.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.h2.clausulas: Main requirements of ISO 22301: the 10 clauses
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.p1: ISO 22301:2019 is structured in ten clauses. The first three (1 to 3) are introductory and do not generate directly auditable requirements. The following seven clauses (4 to 10) contain all the requirements of the Management System:
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c4: Clause 4 — Context of the organisation: the organisation must understand its internal and external context, identify relevant interested parties and their continuity expectations, and define the scope of the BCMS. This clause is the starting point: without a well-defined scope based on the organisation's real context, the rest of the system has no solid foundation.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c5: Clause 5 — Leadership: top management must demonstrate active commitment to the BCMS: assigning clear roles and responsibilities, approving the business continuity policy, ensuring necessary resources and promoting a continuity culture throughout the organisation. It is not enough for a continuity officer to exist; the standard requires real and verifiable involvement at the highest levels.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c6: Clause 6 — Planning: includes the identification and assessment of risks and opportunities for the BCMS, measurable continuity objectives and planning of changes needed to achieve them. Objectives must be aligned with business needs and consistent with the results of the BIA.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c7: Clause 7 — Support: defines the necessary human, technological and infrastructural resources, the competencies of involved personnel, awareness and training, internal and external communication mechanisms during a crisis, and documented management requirements. BCMS documentation is a fundamental pillar of the audit.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c8: Clause 8 — Operation: it is the most extensive and demanding clause. It contains all operational requirements of the BCMS: Business Impact Analysis (BIA), continuity risk assessment, definition of strategies and solutions, development of plans (BCP, DRP, Crisis Management Plan) and requirements for exercises, testing and periodic updating of plans.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c9: Clause 9 — Performance evaluation: the organisation must monitor and measure the performance of the BCMS, conduct periodic internal audits and execute formal management reviews. It is the mechanism that ensures the system does not remain on paper and continually improves based on the results obtained.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.clausulas.c10: Clause 10 — Improvement: defines how to manage identified non-conformities (in audits, exercises or real incidents), associated corrective actions and continual improvement processes of the BCMS. Each incident or drill that reveals weaknesses must translate into documented and verified improvement actions.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.diferenciaIso27001.p1: This is one of the most frequent questions among security and compliance managers. Both standards are complementary and share the same high-level structure, but have different management objects and foci:
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.diferenciaIso27001.p2: ISO 27001 manages information security. Its objective is to protect the confidentiality, integrity and availability of information assets against threats. It covers a broad spectrum of security controls (93 controls in Annex A of ISO 27001:2022, organised in four domains) ranging from cryptography to physical security, access management or secure software development. Its scope is information and the systems that process it.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.diferenciaIso27001.p3: ISO 22301 manages the operational continuity of business processes. Its objective is to ensure that the organisation can continue functioning during and after a disruption, regardless of the cause: not only cyber, but also physical, natural, human or supply chain. It focuses on business processes and the organisation's ability to maintain or restore its critical functions within predefined times.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.diferenciaIso27001.p4: The intersection between the two standards is significant. The availability of information systems is both an objective of ISO 27001 and a central element of ISO 22301 continuity plans. Control A.5.30 of ISO 27001:2022 explicitly addresses ICT preparedness for business continuity and refers to ISO 22301 as the reference standard. In practice, many organisations implement both in an integrated manner, leveraging common documentation, risk management and governance structure to reduce total effort.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.cta2.title: ISO 22301 and ISO 27001 at the same time?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.cta2.subtitle: Our cascade certification packs reduce time and cost by up to 40%.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.sinergiaEns.p1: If your organisation is already certified in the National Security Scheme (ENS) of medium or high category, you have a considerable advantage when approaching ISO 22301. Comparative analysis of the controls of both frameworks reveals that approximately 55% of ISO 22301 requirements are covered, totally or partially, by the controls you should already have implemented under ENS.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.sinergiaEns.p2: The most relevant overlaps occur in several key areas. The ENS organisational framework — security policy, internal regulations, roles and responsibilities, training and awareness — directly meets the requirements of clauses 5 and 7 of ISO 22301 on leadership and support. The operational measures of ENS related to service continuity (op.cont family of Annex II of RD 311/2022) include impact analysis, continuity plan, periodic testing and recovery plan, which correspond directly to the central requirements of clause 8 of ISO 22301.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.sinergiaEns.p3: Similarly, ENS incident management (op.exp.7) and detection and response controls are equivalent to ISO 22301 requirements on plan activation and crisis management. The structured risk management that ENS requires (op.pl.1) provides a directly reusable base for continuity risk analysis.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.sinergiaEns.p4: The additional work to reach ISO 22301 from an ENS base is concentrated in three specific areas: the formal BIA with explicit RTOs and RPOs by business process, continuity plans with the operational level of detail required by ISO 22301 (more granular and process-oriented than ENS), and evidence of exercises and tests with the records required by the international standard. This synergy is the basis of the cascade certification approach that significantly reduces project time and cost compared to an implementation from scratch.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.h2.comoObtener: How to obtain ISO 22301 certification in Spain: step-by-step process
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.p1: The ISO 22301 certification process has well-defined phases. With the right support, the complete process can be completed in 3-4 months:
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li1: Initial GAP analysis: the organisation's current state is assessed against the requirements of ISO 22301. Gaps in documentation, processes, plans and continuity culture are identified. This diagnosis determines the real scope of the project and allows the required effort and cost to be estimated.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li2: Scope and context definition: it is defined which processes, services and organisational units fall within the scope of the BCMS. A well-defined scope is crucial: too broad makes the project expensive; too narrow may not satisfy the contractual or regulatory requirements that motivated the certification.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li3: BIA (Business Impact Analysis): central piece of the project. The owners of each process are interviewed to determine the impact of its interruption over time and the RTO and RPO are established for each critical process. The BIA guides all subsequent continuity strategy decisions.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li4: Continuity risk assessment: threats that could interrupt critical processes are identified and their probability and impact are assessed. The most critical risks determine investment priorities in continuity strategies.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li5: Definition of continuity strategies: for each critical process and each relevant risk scenario, response strategies are defined: manual or remote operation alternatives, alternative suppliers, backup sites, data replication systems, etc.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li6: Plan development: the Business Continuity Plan (BCP), Disaster Recovery Plan (DRP) and Crisis Management Plan are documented. These documents must be operational: step-by-step action scripts, with clearly defined owners, resources and activation criteria.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li7: Exercises and tests: ISO 22301 requires that plans be tested before the audit. Exercises can be walk-throughs, functional drills or real technical tests. Each exercise must generate a report with results and identified improvement actions.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li8: Internal audit and management review: the conformity of the BCMS is verified before the external audit, and top management formally reviews the state of the system and approves the necessary improvement decisions.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li9: Certification audit (Phase 1 and Phase 2): the certification body first performs a documentary audit (Phase 1) to verify that the BCMS documentation conforms to the standard. Subsequently, it performs the implementation audit (Phase 2), verifying that the documented controls are actually functioning.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.comoObtener.li10: Certificate issuance: once the identified non-conformities are closed, the ISO 22301 certificate is issued with a validity of 3 years, subject to annual follow-up audits.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.h2.tiempoCoste: Time and cost of ISO 22301 certification
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.tiempoCoste.p1: The time required to get ISO 22301 certified depends on several factors: the initial maturity of the organisation in continuity matters, the scope of the BCMS, the size of the company and the availability of the internal team to participate in the project.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.tiempoCoste.p2: Organisations with no prior continuity management: the process from scratch to certificate usually takes between 6 and 12 months with internal resources, or between 3 and 5 months with the support of a specialised consultancy. The difference lies in methodological efficiency: a consultancy with experience in ISO 22301 has templates, methodologies and an optimised process that avoids the errors and rework that lengthen internal projects.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.tiempoCoste.p3: Organisations with prior ENS or ISO 27001: thanks to control synergy, time is reduced to 2-3 months in many cases. The reuse of risk management, documentary structure and already implemented continuity controls considerably accelerates the project without sacrificing implementation quality.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.tiempoCoste.p4: Regarding cost, three main items are involved: consultancy fees (implementation of the BCMS, preparation of the BIA, plans and training), fees of the certification body (Phase 1, Phase 2 and annual follow-up audits), and internal team dedication costs. Investment varies according to the size and complexity of the organisation. As a reference of value, the cost of certification is usually lower than the economic impact of a single unmanaged major disruption.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.cta3.title: Do you need ISO 22301 certification?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.cta3.subtitle: 100% success guarantee in writing. Average time: 3-4 months.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.a1: ISO 22301 is not a generally legally binding standard in Spain. However, certain sectors such as financial (DORA), critical infrastructure (PIC Law) or entities subject to ENS of medium or high category strongly require or recommend it. Many public tenders and corporate contracts incorporate it as a requirement or evaluation criterion, so in practice it can be mandatory to access certain markets.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.q2: How long does ISO 22301 certification take?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.a2: With the support of a specialised consultancy, the process from diagnosis to certificate takes between 3 and 4 months. Without expert advice, the timeline can extend to 9-12 months, mainly due to the complexity of the BIA, the development of operational continuity plans and the preparation for the exercises and tests required by the standard. The most critical point in time is the BIA, which requires interviewing the owners of all critical processes and building the impact analyses from scratch.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.q3: Are ISO 22301 and business continuity the same?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.a3: Not exactly. Business continuity is the management discipline oriented to maintaining operations during disruptions. ISO 22301 is the international standard that standardises how to implement and certify a Business Continuity Management System (BCMS). Having a continuity plan does not mean being ISO 22301 certified; the standard requires a systemic approach with a PDCA lifecycle, auditable evidence of implementation, periodic documented testing and verifiable continual improvement.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.q4: What is the BIA in ISO 22301?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.a4: The BIA (Business Impact Analysis) is the central analysis of ISO 22301. It consists of identifying the organisation's critical processes, determining the impact their interruption would cause over time — financial, reputational, legal, operational — and establishing recovery parameters: RTO (Recovery Time Objective, maximum tolerable recovery time before impact becomes unacceptable) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective, maximum tolerable data loss). The BIA is the basis on which all continuity strategies and plans are designed.
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.q5: If I already have ENS, how much work is left for ISO 22301?
blog/que-es-iso22301.article.faq.a5: If your organisation is already certified in ENS of medium or high category, you have covered approximately 55% of ISO 22301 requirements. The incident management, service continuity, risk management and documentary structure controls of ENS are directly reusable. The additional work is concentrated on the formal BIA with explicit RTOs and RPOs by process, continuity plans with the operational level of detail required by ISO 22301 and documented evidence of periodic exercises and tests.
